There are two modes of war: either the two combatants are at a distance from one another, throwing stones, arrows, and catapults at each other; or the two combatants approach one another (in physical proximity) until they struggle with each other—"and a man struggled with him"—where they draw close (not only in place, but) also with their bodies, until the two combatants embrace one another. This war (the ultimate closeness) is the most difficult war.

Similarly, the "peace" in this verse is not merely peace, but the ultimate perfection of peace. As the Baal HaGeulah explains in the maamar, the true meaning of "He redeemed my soul in peace" is that those who were previously enemies and adversaries "become complete lovers." This state is even higher than the peace that existed in the days of Shlomo. The peace in Shlomo’s days was that everyone was nullified due to the revelation of light (but they were not transformed into holiness). The true essence of peace—where even the adversary is transformed into holiness—will be in the future, as it is written: "Then I will turn to the peoples a clear language... to serve Him with one shoulder." Similarly regarding animals: "The wolf shall dwell with the lamb," and even the snake (the viper and the flying serpent) will be transformed into good, as it is written: "Also his enemies shall make peace with him"—this refers to the snake—just as it was at the beginning of creation (before the sin of the Tree of Knowledge), when there was a "great sun." Furthermore, since every descent is for the sake of an ascent, it must be said that the clarification and elevation of the snake in the future will be even higher than the level of the "great sun" that existed before the descent. Based on this, one must understand even more clearly the phrase "He redeemed in peace... from the battle against me," for the battle and the peace in this verse are two opposites from one extreme to the other. How, then, is it a "redemption in peace from the battle"?

3. This can be understood by prefacing what is explained in the maamar of the Baal HaGeulah: that both concepts, battle and peace, exist in the service of *tefillah*. It should be noted that it is explained in many places that the clarification of *tefillah* is through the path of war—"the time of prayer is a time of battle"—while the clarification through the path of rest (peace) is through the study of Torah. However, in this *drush* of the Baal HaGeulah, it is explained that both concepts exist even within the service of *tefillah*.

The love of "with all your heart," which is from the level of *nefesh, ruach, neshamah*, involves the fact that even though through this love one causes the *nefesh habehamis* to also have a love for Hashem—"with all your heart, with both your inclinations"—nevertheless, since the love of "with all your heart" is based on understanding and comprehension (that *Elokus* is good), and since intellect is an internal quality (it is internalized within the thing it affects), it is understood that the action of the *nefesh elokis* upon the *nefesh habehamis* to love Hashem is through the *nefesh elokis* becoming "clothed" within the *nefesh habehamis*. The contemplation of the *nefesh elokis* must be in such matters and in such a way that the *nefesh habehamis* can also grasp it. This "clothing" (the *nefesh elokis* clothing itself in the *nefesh habehamis*) is a matter of war.

Even the love of "with all your soul," which is from the level of *chayah*—even though *chayah* is a level of *makif* (encompassing light), and the clarification of the *nefesh habehamis* through this love is not through "clothing" but rather that through the intensity of the will of the *nefesh elokis* (a will that is above reason), the desires of the *nefesh habehamis* are nullified of their own accord—nevertheless, since the nullification of the desires of the *nefesh habehamis* is only due to the overcoming power of the will of the *nefesh elokis* (meaning that even now, the *nefesh habehamis* still has foreign desires in a hidden state, but they do not come into actuality due to the strength of the *nefesh elokis*), this is still included in the service of clarification that is through the path of war.

The concept of "redeemed in peace" is in the love of "with all your might," which is from the level of *yechidah*. Because the level of *yechidah* has no *sitra achra* opposite it—"the shadow of the shadow has no power"—therefore, when the level of *yechidah* is revealed, the *sitra achra* is nullified. The level of *chayah*, because it does have a *sitra achra* opposite it, does not negate and nullify the foreign desires of the *nefesh habehamis*; it only acts through the path of overcoming, where the desire for *Elokus* (from the level of *chayah*) overcomes the desires of the *nefesh habehamis*. For the desires of the *nefesh habehamis* to be nullified, it requires the revelation of the *yechidah*.

4. This can be connected to what is known regarding the power of *mesiras nefesh* in every Jew (that the power of *mesiras nefesh* is from the *yechidah*). The fact that he gives his soul so as not to be separated, Heaven forbid, from His unity, is not because of his own will (i.e., that it is possible to be otherwise, but he chooses this way), but because he *cannot* be separated, Heaven forbid, from His unity. Based on this, we can explain why it is stated in many places that the love of "with all your might" is the concept of *mesiras nefesh*. Seemingly, this is difficult to understand: does the love of "with all your heart" and "with all your soul" not also necessitate *mesiras nefesh*? When one loves Hashem with all his heart, and certainly when his love for Hashem is with all his soul, he would surely give his soul for Him! What is the elevation and novelty in the love of "with all your might"?

The explanation is that the *mesiras nefesh* from the love of "with all your heart" and "with all your soul" is in a way where he *wants* to give his soul for Hashem (in the love of "with all your heart," *nefesh, ruach, neshamah*—a will according to reason; and in the love of "with all your soul," *chayah*—a will above reason). In contrast, the *mesiras nefesh* from the love of "with all your might," *yechidah*, is in a way where it is *impossible* to be separated, Heaven forbid. This is why the concept of *mesiras nefesh* is in the love of "with all your might," because *mesiras nefesh* is the concept of *bitul* (nullification), and the true essence of *bitul* is when the *mesiras nefesh* is not from his own will (his own existence), but from the side of *Elokus*. It can be said that this is also one of the explanations for why the nullification of the foreign desires of the *nefesh habehamis* occurs specifically through the love of "with all your might." Because with the love of "with all your heart" and "with all your soul," since there is a possibility of not giving one's soul (and the fact that he does is only due to his will), this proves that the existence of the *sitra achra* still remains. The nullification of the *sitra achra* (complete nullification) is through the love of "with all your might," because with this love, it is *impossible* to be otherwise, Heaven forbid.

5. In the aforementioned maamar of the Baal HaGeulah, it is explained at the beginning that the war (of the *nefesh elokis* with the *nefesh habehamis*) is in the *nefesh, ruach, neshamah* that are clothed in the body and the *nefesh habehamis*, whereas in the *makifim* of *chayah* and *yechidah*, war is not applicable. However, later in the continuation of the maamar, he explains in greater detail that even in the level of *chayah* there is a matter of war, and the matter of peace is specifically in the *yechidah*. This can be compared to what Hashem said to King David (when he wanted to build the Beis Hamikdash): "You have shed much blood... you shall not build a house for My name... behold, a son shall be born to you, he shall be a man of rest... he shall build a house for My name." Yet, the design of the Beis Hamikdash was made by David ("The hand of Hashem was upon me, giving me insight"), and David gave to his son Shlomo the plan of the hall, etc. One must understand: since the Beis Hamikdash is connected specifically with rest and peace, which is why it was said to David, "You shall not build a house for My name because you are a man of war," why were the preparations for the Beis Hamikdash made by David, who was a man of war?

The explanation is that at that time (when David wanted to build the Beis Hamikdash, and even more so when he prepared the design), David was in a state of rest, and Hashem had given him rest from all his enemies round about. However, since David’s rest came about through the wars he had waged, the building of the Beis Hamikdash had to be by Shlomo, a man of rest—rest in its essence. It can be said that what is explained in the maamar (at the beginning) that even in the level of *chayah* war is not applicable, is like the rest of David after he had won all the wars. In the level of *nefesh, ruach, neshamah* that are clothed in the body and *nefesh habehamis*, since the action of the *nefesh elokis* upon the *nefesh habehamis* is through the path of clothing and engagement—as the *nefesh habehamis* finds its grasp in the good of physical things, and the *nefesh elokis* engages with it to explain to it the good of *Elokus*—this is a matter of actual war. In contrast, the level of *chayah*, since its action upon the *nefesh habehamis* is (not through clothing, but) that through the revelation of the intensity of the will for *Elokus* of the *nefesh elokis*, the desires of the *nefesh habehamis* are nullified of their own accord, this is not a matter of war. In general, it is a matter of rest. However, this rest is (not rest in its essence, but) only due to the overcoming of the will of the *nefesh elokis*, similar to the rest of David after Hashem gave him rest from all his enemies, where his rest was due to the fact that he had won all the wars. In contrast, the nullification of the desires of the *nefesh habehamis* through the revelation of the *yechidah* is (not that through the revelation of the *yechidah* the desires *become* nullified, but) that from the side of the *yechidah* (which has no *sitra achra* opposite it), there is no place for foreign desires from the outset, similar to the rest of Shlomo, which was (not through victory, but) rest in its essence.

6. Similarly, this applies to the service that is through the path of war, which has many levels. The war in *tefillah*—"the time of prayer is a time of battle"—since at the time of prayer one is detached from the matters of the world and his engagement is to contemplate matters of *Elokus* and to arouse the love and fear of Hashem, but the contemplation (and the love and fear) must be in a way that it is also graspable in the intellect of the *nefesh habehamis*, this is not actual war (and is called the "sword" of peace). The service through the path of war is primarily at the time of eating and drinking—"eat bread at the edge of the sword"—and similarly in other bodily needs. Within this itself, the main war is at the time of engaging in business and commerce. For the war of eating (and similarly in other bodily needs) is that his eating should be for the sake of Heaven and not to fulfill the desires of his soul—meaning he fights *against* the desire and pleasure in the food (and is not clothed in it). In contrast, engagement in business must be (according to Torah) through the path of clothing: "You have dealt with faith." [It can be said that this is also the reason why the general process of clarifications is called *Canaan*, for *Canaan* means a merchant, because the essence of the clarifications that are through the path of clothing is in business.] Thus, the war of eating is like the war with a sword ("eat bread at the edge of the *sword*"), for although the war of the sword is specifically when one is in proximity to the enemy (this is the difference between a sword and a bow), this proximity is only in place. In contrast, the war in business is like the war called *kerav*, from the root *kiruv*, because the *kiruv* of *kerav* is (not only in place, but) that the two combatants embrace each other, as explained at length above (section 2).

7. This is "He redeemed my soul in peace from the battle against me"—that even the service of clarifications that is through the path of *kerav* (and not just through the path of war, but through the path of *kerav*) should be in a way of "redeemed in peace," peace in the ultimate perfection, like the peace that was in the days of Shlomo, and even higher—like the peace that will be in the future. The reason for this is as the verse continues: "for there were many with me." Through the service of "there were many with me," which is the arousal and revelation of the *yechidah*—as explained at length in the aforementioned *drush* of the Baal HaGeulah, that every Jew has the power to arouse and reveal the *yechidah* within him—through this, "He redeemed my soul in peace" is accomplished. The soul of the person serving—even though he engages in the matters of the world in order to clarify them in a way of *kerav*—nevertheless, he will not be caught in it, and his redemption will be in peace, leading to peace in the ultimate perfection. Furthermore, even the redemption of the sparks that are in the matters of the world will be in peace, "for there were many with me," that even the sparks that were initially in the category of "many" (because they were in exile in the public domain, the "mountains of separation") are included in the soul of the person clarifying them—"with me"—until they are included in the *yechidah* within him.

8. It can be said that this is also the connection and relevance of the redemption of 10 Kislev and 19 Kislev to the verse "He redeemed my soul in peace." Even though the redemption was from a *kerav* (battle), including the fact that the imprisonment came through the denunciation of those who had a relationship of *kiruv* (closeness)—which (the fact that the imprisonment came through them) is a great descent and sorrow—nevertheless, the redemption was in peace and perfection, to the point that it became a prelude and preparation for the ultimate perfection that will be in the future. Through "your wellsprings shall spread outward," which began primarily on 19 Kislev, and especially through the service of the Baal HaGeulah of 10 Kislev, whose spreading of the wellsprings was in the way of "the broad rivers"—meaning he drew forth the *wellspring* in the way of broad rivers—through this, "the Master, the King Moshiach, will come," when there will be true peace and tranquility in the ultimate perfection. "Those who taste it have merited life," for they begin to taste a sample of this even in the final days of exile, as they are able to engage in the service of "your wellsprings shall spread outward" from a place of rest, joy, and goodness of heart, in a way of "increasing and ascending in light." And very soon we will merit that "the night shall shine as the day," with the coming of our righteous Moshiach.

## 10 Kislev 5741

"He has redeemed my soul in peace from the battle against me, for there were many with me". The Baal HaGeulah explains in his maamar on this subject (printed in his sefer *Shaarei Teshuvah*) that the interpretation of "redeemed in peace" (specifically in peace) is that the redemption occurs in such a way that the adversary is completely nullified, which is why there is no need for battle at all. This is similar to what occurred in the days of Shlomo, when all the nations were nullified before Shlomo without the need for war; this is why he was called Shlomo, for there was *shalom* (peace) in his days, and so on. However, the peace of Shlomo’s days was not yet complete. The ultimate perfection of this will be in the days of Moshiach, when evil will be completely nullified (as it is written, "And I will remove the spirit of impurity from the land"). Therefore, at that time, "all the nations will stream to him" (they will stream of their own accord). The same applies to animals—"they will not harm or destroy", and "the lion will eat straw like the ox". Perhaps it can be said that from the fact that the maamar cites the verse "the lion will eat straw like the ox," it is understood that in the days of Moshiach themselves, the perfection of "redeemed in peace" will be specifically in the second era. As is explained elsewhere, reconciling the opinions regarding the interpretation of "the wolf will dwell with the lamb" : in the first era of the days of Moshiach, this will be by way of "parable and riddle", referring to the nations of the world, while in the second era, it will be literal (regarding animals).

2) The explanation of this matter (that although the concept of redemption in peace existed in the days of Shlomo, the ultimate perfection of it will be in the days of Moshiach, and within them, specifically in the second era) can be understood from what is explained in the maamar : that even through the study of Torah (now), a redemption in peace is effected, because the *birur* (refinement) achieved through Torah study is "the essence of the nullification of evil." As is explained in several *derushim* regarding the difference between Torah and *tefillah*: the *birur* of *tefillah* is by way of battle—"the time of prayer is a time of war" —whereas the *birur* of Torah is by way of tranquility. The difference between a *birur* through battle and a *birur* through tranquility is that in a *birur* through battle, the one performing the *birur* must clothe himself in the garments of that which is being refined. This is not the case in a *birur* through tranquility; the one performing the *birur* remains in his place, and through the revelation of the light (of the one performing the *birur*) shining upon that which is being refined, it is refined automatically. This is how it was in the days of Shlomo: Shlomo remained in his place, and through the greatness of his name, which was heard even in distant places, they were drawn to him of their own accord, as it is written, "And the Queen of Sheba heard the fame of Shlomo... and she came to Jerusalem." This is the same difference between the *birur* of *tefillah* and the *birur* of Torah: the *birur* of *tefillah* is in a manner where the *nefesh elokis* clothes itself in the *nefesh habahamit* (and as is known, in order for the *hisbonenus* of *tefillah* to effect a *birur* of the *nefesh habahamit*, the *hisbonenus* must be in such matters and in such a way that the *nefesh habahamit* can also grasp it). This is not the case with the *birur* of Torah, which is automatic; by studying in the Torah that this thing is permitted and that thing is forbidden, they are refined automatically.

3) Now, within the *birur* of Torah, there is a difference between the *birur* through *galya d'Torah* (the revealed Torah) and the *birur* through *pnimiyus haTorah* (the inner dimension of Torah). In the *birur* through the study of *galya d'Torah*, the study must be in those very matters that need to be refined. The reasons for this—that the Torah clothed itself in physical matters, and even in forbidden matters, and even in arguments of falsehood —is in order to effect the *birur* even in these matters. The reason that the *birur* through *galya d'Torah* is also called a *birur* by way of tranquility is because even these matters (forbidden things and arguments of falsehood), as they are in the Torah, are *Torah*—Torah of truth. And since even these things in the Torah are the word of Hashem —"Are not My words like fire", and just as fire... so too words of Torah cannot receive impurity —the Torah is always in its place and its level (the word of Hashem), and the *birur* is automatic. Nevertheless, this is not comparable to the *birur* through *pnimiyus haTorah*. For in the *birur* through *galya d'Torah*, the very fact that the Torah speaks regarding physical matters, etc., is a matter of descent, so to speak, even though after its descent and its being drawn downward, it remains Torah, the word of Hashem (and it is not that it descends and clothes itself in another matter). This is not the case with the *birur* through *pnimiyus haTorah*; since *pnimiyus haTorah* is the "Tree of Life" (which is not clothed in good and evil), the *birur* effected through it is not by way of clothing at all.

4) Perhaps it can be added regarding the difference between *galya d'Torah* and *pnimiyus haTorah* that the true essence of tranquility and peace is specifically in *pnimiyus haTorah*. This is not only regarding the *birurim* through Torah study, but even in the study of Torah itself. The study of *nigleh* (the revealed) Torah is in a manner of questions and answers: initially, it is thought that the logic is such-and-such, and afterward, one arrives at a deeper understanding and rejects the previous logic, to the point where one says of it, "it is a fabrication". The rejection of the previous logic is the concept of "you have shed much blood" (which was said regarding Dovid) as it exists in the world of intellect. The true essence of peace is specifically in *pnimiyus haTorah*, where "there is neither question nor dispute". It can be said that this difference between *galya d'Torah* and *pnimiyus haTorah* in the very manner of study is the cause of the difference between them regarding the *birur* they effect. For every agent acts according to its own likeness; therefore, specifically *pnimiyus haTorah*, in which there is neither question nor dispute, also effects its *birur* by way of peace and tranquility.

5) Now, even within *pnimiyus haTorah* itself, there are two general modes. There is the mode in which it is clothed in the understanding and comprehension of the intellect; this aspect of *pnimiyus haTorah* exists even now. And there is the aspect of "seeing" (transcending understanding and comprehension), which will exist in the future. As is known that Moshiach will teach (the *pnimiyus* of) Torah to all of Israel in a manner of "seeing." The difference between comprehension and seeing is that in comprehension, even when it is verified, it is possible that through a question, there could be a change or at least a weakening. This is not the case in "seeing"; even if the strongest questions are posed to him, there will be no doubt in his mind regarding what he has seen. The reason for this is that when a person comprehends something with his intellect, it is an external addition to him. This is not the case when he sees something; the thing is engraved within his inner being, as if it has become part of his very essence, and therefore no change is possible.

6) Based on all the above, it can be explained that although the concept of "redeemed in peace" existed in the days of Shlomo, the perfection of it will be in the days of Moshiach, and within the days of Moshiach, in the second era. It can be said that these three levels of peace (in the days of Shlomo, in the first era of the days of Moshiach, and in the second era) are analogous to the three levels in Torah mentioned above. The matter is that the fact that the Queen of Sheba heard the fame of Shlomo was because his name (a name is a *ha'arah*, a radiation) was drawn and spread to distant places, as far as the land of Sheba. Shlomo himself remained in his place, and his name ("the fame of Shlomo") was drawn and reached Sheba, and the further the drawing reached, the more it diminished. Similarly, regarding the *birur* itself: even after the Queen of Sheba heard the fame of Shlomo, it was still necessary that "she came to Jerusalem" (the journey from Sheba to Jerusalem took time and effort, etc.), and only then was the *birur* effected (analogous to the *birur* through *galya d'Torah*). This is not the case with the *birur* that will be in the days of Moshiach; since at that time there will be the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* as it was before the *Tzimtzum*, and this light is equal in every place, therefore the *birur* at that time will be (not in a manner of drawing and descending the light downward and elevating that which is being refined upward, but rather) that the light as it is will shine in every place. Therefore, at that time, "I will turn to the nations (as they are in their reality and their place)... all of them... to serve Him with one shoulder."

However, within this itself, there is a difference between the first era and the second era. In the first era, there will be no change in the nature of the world; the revelation of *Elokus* that will be in the world will be from the perspective of the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that shines in every place, but not from the perspective of the world itself, since "the world follows its natural course." Therefore, the revelation of the created beings (and their nullification) will be like something added to their existence. [Analogous to the understanding and comprehension of *pnimiyus haTorah*, which is like something added to the person, which is why change or at least weakening is possible, as mentioned above]. This is not the case in the second era of the days of Moshiach, when there will be a *change and innovation* in the nature of the world; it can be said that the revelation in the world (and the nullification of the created beings) will be also from the perspective of the nature and definition of the world itself.

7) Now, the three interpretations (and levels) mentioned above of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" are all related to one another because they are all in the same verse, and they come in one sequence. Beyond the fact that all the levels are Torah [just as in *tefillah*, even the *tefillah* of Shabbos—although the *birur* of Shabbos is by way of tranquility, nevertheless, the *tefillah* of Shabbos is still *tefillah*—so too regarding Torah: even *galya d'Torah*, which is in a state of clothing, is still *Torah*], all the levels of Torah are one matter, one Torah. Therefore, through the study of Torah now (even the study of *nigleh*), one will merit the Torah of Moshiach, which is of the level of "seeing," as is known regarding the matter of "his study is in his hand".

8) Perhaps it can be said with greater depth that even the level of redemption that is by way of battle, since it is called "redemption," is related to "redemption in peace" (up to the most exalted level that will be in the days of Moshiach in the second era). In the words of Chazal, "War is also the beginning of redemption," because the name of redemption is upon it. Based on this, we can explain further the connection of the redemption of 10 Kislev and 19 Kislev with the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace". Seemingly, the course of events in the redemption of the Alter Rebbe, and similarly in the redemption of the Mitteler Rebbe, involved matters of *iskafya* (coercion), effort, and the like—that is, matters of battle. Yet they connect this to the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace," which refers (as the Mitteler Rebbe explains in the aforementioned *derush*) to redemption in peace in its ultimate perfection. The explanation for this is that all levels of redemption are connected to the redemption in peace in its ultimate perfection that will be in the future. This can be understood based on what the Baal HaGeulah explains in the maamar : that the concept of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" in the *avodah* of man (that the redemption of the *nefesh elokis* from the exile of the *nefesh habahamit* is in a manner of peace, where the *nefesh habahamit* does not oppose at all) is when the *avodah* is with *mesiras nefesh*, and within *mesiras nefesh* itself—the *mesiras nefesh* of the *yechidah*. Through the *mesiras nefesh* that comes from the *chayah*, only an *iskafya* of the *nefesh habahamit* is effected, but through the *mesiras nefesh* that comes specifically from the *yechidah*, it is completely nullified. Based on this, the connection of the redemption that is by way of battle (the *avodah* from the level of *chayah*, and lower still, the *avodah* from the level of *nefesh-ruach-neshamah*) to "He has redeemed my soul in peace" (*yechidah*) is understood, for in every *avodah*, there is (in a hidden way) the *mesiras nefesh* that comes from the *yechidah*. As it is stated in *Tanya* that the study of Torah, the performance of *mitzvos*, and *tefillah* are matters of actual *mesiras nefesh*. Since the *true* essence of *mesiras nefesh* (*actual* *mesiras nefesh*) is the *mesiras nefesh* that comes from the *yechidah*, it follows that in every matter of Torah and *mitzvos* [even the simple matters of Torah and *mitzvos* which, had the Torah not been given (Heaven forbid), we would have learned from the beasts of the earth], there is the power of the *mesiras nefesh* of the *yechidah*. Furthermore, even the understanding and comprehension of Torah is through the concept of "we will do" preceding "we will hear," as it is written, "The integrity of the upright will guide them". The concept of "we will do" preceding "we will hear" is the nullification to the Owner of the Will (which is higher than the nullification to the Will itself), and this nullification comes from the essence of the soul (which is higher than the revelations of the soul), the level of *yechidah*.

Although the concept of *mesiras nefesh* explained in the aforementioned maamar of the Baal HaGeulah is that all his desires are nullified (to the point that all matters are in a state of equality), it can be said that all levels of *mesiras nefesh* are one matter. Similar to what is known regarding the concept of *yirah* (fear/awe): since *yirah* is nullification (the negation of existence), all levels of *yirah* are one *yirah*. So too, it can be said regarding the nullification of *mesiras nefesh*. On the contrary, since the nullification of *mesiras nefesh* is from the perspective of the totality and essence of the soul, unlike the nullification of *yirah* which is only one attribute, this concept [that all levels are one matter] in *mesiras nefesh* is even more so than in *yirah*. This can be understood by way of a parable from the kingdom of the earth : when a person enters the army (even though he has not yet served in the army, and certainly has not been in battle), he surrenders himself completely and "is no longer the master of himself." Similarly, it is in the spiritual army, for the Jewish people are the "armies of Hashem". The fact that every Jew has *kabbalas ol* (acceptance of the yoke)—as the well-known saying goes, "A Yid is a *kabbalas-ol*nik"—by which he enters the armies of Hashem, through this he surrenders himself completely to *Elokus*, and this surrender is connected to, and the *mesiras nefesh* that comes from the *yechidah* shines within it, at the most exalted level.

9) Based on the above, we can also explain that which is found in the *Yerushalmi* on the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace... for there were many with me," that it refers to the men of Avshalom who prayed for the peace of Dovid. Seemingly, it was explained above that redemption in peace is specifically in Torah, whereas *tefillah* is by way of battle, and the *prayer* of the men of Avshalom was regarding the war of Avshalom. Similarly, one must understand the connection of the redemption of 19 Kislev with the verse "He has redeemed in peace." The redemption of the Alter Rebbe occurred (as he writes in his *Iggeres HaKodesh*) when "I read in the Book of Psalms." The concept of reciting Psalms (even though the Book of Psalms is one of the twenty-four books of the *Tanach*, which is Torah) is *tefillah*, and in the words of the verse, "the pleasant *songs* of Israel" (for songs are related to *tefillah*). This is especially so based on what is known from the stories of our Rebbes, the Nesi'im, that the redemption of the Alter Rebbe came through the prayers of his students and Chassidim and all those who follow in his footsteps. And this redemption, which came through *tefillah*—"the time of prayer is a time of war"—came through the recitation of the verse "He has redeemed in peace." The explanation for this is that since the study of Torah and *mitzvos* is a matter of actual *mesiras nefesh* (as mentioned above from *Tanya*), therefore, *tefillah* is also connected to "He has redeemed in peace." It can be said that, on the contrary, the concept of *mesiras nefesh* is in *tefillah* even more than in Torah. For the fact that Torah study is actual *mesiras nefesh*, this feeling of *mesiras nefesh* must be "before he begins to study" [and when he studies for many consecutive hours, he must contemplate this every single hour], but at the time of study, when he is engaged in the understanding and comprehension of the matter he is studying, he does not feel the nullification of *mesiras nefesh*. On the contrary, "Who is the King? The Sages," and "it is the Torah that burns within him". This is not the case in *tefillah*, where one must be "like a servant before his master", in a state of ultimate nullification (the nullification of *mesiras nefesh*).

10) Based on all the above, it is understood why specifically through the *avodah* now, in the time of the "footsteps of Moshiach" when "darkness covers the earth", we arrive at the revelations of the future redemption. For specifically in this time, when one must engage with the sparks of holiness that have fallen into an impure place, and one must turn bitterness into sweetness and darkness into light, and even when from time to time one exits the "four cubits of Torah" or the "four cubits of *tefillah*"—to leave the *beis knesses* and the *beis medrash*—and in the words of the *Shulchan Aruch*, "conduct yourself in them with the conduct of the way of the land", nevertheless, through the *avodah* now, specifically, we bring the future redemption. At that time, what is written will be fulfilled: "And you will be gathered one by one," each and every one of Israel and all of Israel, "and their silver and their gold with them", with all the sparks of holiness belonging to them, and also those found in their silver and gold literally. This is because in this *avodah* during the time of exile, there is the concept of *mesiras nefesh* in its ultimate form. As is known from the teaching of our Rebbe the Alter Rebbe (and at length in the *maamarim* of the Nasi of our generation, the Rebbe) on the verse, "And the man Moshe was very humble, more than any man on the face of the earth," that the humility of Moshe (Moshe is truth and his Torah is truth) was especially in connection to the generation of the "footsteps of Moshiach," seeing their *mesiras nefesh* in the fulfillment of Torah and *mitzvos* in the matter of "do not be ashamed before those who mock", etc., and the like. This leads to the fulfillment of the prophecy, "And the humble will increase their joy in Hashem", "everlasting joy upon their heads" —the joy of the true and complete redemption, which comes especially through "spreading the wellsprings outward", and in a manner of "being redeemed immediately", and in a manner of "redeemed in peace," without any effort, until the prophecy is fulfilled, "And kings will be your foster fathers and their queens your nursing mothers," and "they will bring all your brothers... as a tribute to Hashem... in a pure vessel," very soon, in the final days of exile, and afterward, immediately and in perfection, in actual fact, in the true and complete redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, speedily and truly in our days.

## B’s”d. Shabbos Parshas Vayeitzei, 9 Kislev, Erev 10 Kislev, 5742

1. "You are One, and Your Name is One, and who is like Your people Israel, one nation on earth," etc. The Mitteler Rebbe, the *baal ha-hillula* of 9 Kislev (and the *baal ha-geulah* of 10 Kislev), explains in his *maamar* entitled "Zeh" [which appears to be the *drush* "Atah Echad" that he delivered on the last Shabbos of his imprisonment (Shabbos Parshas Vayeitzei, 9 Kislev, 5587) before Mincha], that this entire passage explains the three *kavim* (lines) of *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* four times, corresponding to the four levels that encompass the entire *seder hishtalshelus*, beginning from before the *Tzimtzum*. "You are One, and Your Name is One, and who is like Your people Israel, one nation on earth" are the three *kavim* of *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* as they exist before the *Tzimtzum*, for even before the *Tzimtzum* there is a division into three *kavim*, such that the concept of division is relevant even before the *Tzimtzum*. "Great *Tiferes* and crown of salvation, a day of rest and holiness You gave to Your people" refers to the three *kavim* as they exist after the *Tzimtzum*, but in the worlds of *Ein Sof* that are above *Atzilus* and are not in a state of actual limitation (as in *Atzilus*, where there are *keilim* that limit). Therefore, it says regarding this, "You gave to Your people," for "giving" implies that it is transferred from hand to hand without change (between before the *Tzimtzum* and after the *Tzimtzum*). After this ("Avraham will rejoice," etc., "a rest of love and generosity," etc.), it speaks of the three *kavim* as they exist in *Atzilus*, for in *Atzilus* they are in a state of limitation—"ten and not nine, ten and not eleven." Within this itself there are two levels: "Avraham will rejoice, Yitzchak will exult, Yaakov and his children will rest in it" refers to the *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* of *Atzilus*; "a rest of love and generosity, a rest of truth and faith, a rest of peace, tranquility, and security" refers to the *Netzach-Hod-Yesod* of *Atzilus*. This is explained at length in the aforementioned *drush*.

2. It is a well-known precision in the *drushim* of our Rebbes that all matters within them, including the time they are said, are precise. One must connect the four aforementioned levels with the verse (in Parshas Vayeitzei, which is one of the reasons why this *maamar* was said on Shabbos Parshas Vayeitzei): "And behold, a ladder set up on the earth, and its top reached to the heavens." This refers to the ladder of *tefillah*, the "ladder of *tzlusa*," for the ladder of *tefillah* has four rungs. It can be said that these four rungs of *tefillah*—in addition to the explanation that they correspond to the four worlds of *ABYA*—correspond in a general sense to the four aforementioned levels, the entire *seder hishtalshelus*, beginning from before the *Tzimtzum*.

3. The *maamar* continues, stating that what is said, "a complete rest that You desire" (after detailing the three *kavim* of *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* in all four aforementioned levels), is the aspect of the perfection of everything, the simple unity that is above the three *kavim*, even above the source of the three *kavim* as they exist before the *Tzimtzum*. Regarding this it says, "Your children will recognize and know," meaning that your children (specifically souls in bodies) will recognize and know this level as well. The reason for this is "for from You is their rest," for their rest (of Shabbos) is from You, literally from You.

This should be connected with what is known regarding the interpretation of "for from You is their rest": *Me’itcha* (from You) is the letters *me’es kaf* (from the *kaf*), which is *Keter*, from there is their rest. Just as in the *hishtalshelus* of the four worlds of *ABYA*, the beginning of the *hishtalshelus* is from *Atzilus* and *Keter* is above the *hishtalshelus*, so too is it in the *hishtalshelus* of the four aforementioned levels (which are analogous to *ABYA*): the beginning of the *hishtalshelus* is from the (aspect of *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* that is) before the *Tzimtzum* (the aspect of *Atzilus* of the whole), and the aspect of *Keter* (there) is the aspect of *Ein Sof* itself (which is above even the *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* that is in *Ein Sof*), literally from You.

4. The explanation of the matter (that the rest of Shabbos is literally from You) is that the rest of Shabbos is from the essence of the *nefesh*, the aspect of *yechidah*. As explained in the *maamar*, this is the reason why the rest of Shabbos is equal in all Jews, and there is no difference between the greatest of the world and the simplest of the masses (unlike the *tefillah* of Shabbos, and likewise the eating of Shabbos, which in great *tzaddikim* are in a much more elevated manner than in simple people). For in the aspect of *yechidah*, all Jews are equal, and it is found in its perfection in every soul of Israel, even in the lowest and simplest. "And this is 'from You is their rest'": the rest of Shabbos is literally from You, for the revelation of the aspect of the perfection of everything that is above the *kavim* in all four aforementioned levels (even from the *kavim* as they are before the *Tzimtzum*, literally from You) is specifically in the aspect of *yechidah*. For the four aspects of *nefesh-ruach-neshamah-chayah* are in the four worlds of *ABYA*, and higher—in the four levels of the general *hishtalshelus* (beginning from before the *Tzimtzum*)—whereas the aspect of *yechidah* is above the *hishtalshelus* (within man), and it is even above the aspect of the *makif* of the house [for it is explained in many places that the aspect of *chayah* is like the *makif* of the garment, and the aspect of *yechidah* is like the *makif* of the house. But in the aforementioned *drush* "Atah Echad" (and so it is in many places), the aspect of *yechidah* is above even the *makif* of the house. Therefore, even in the house (which is a greater *makif* than the *makif* of the garments), the aspect of *yechidah* will not dwell, but only in the four *amos* that are distant in the extreme, more than the distance of the house—there specifically will the light of the aspect of *yechidah* dwell]. Therefore, specifically in the aspect of *yechidah* is the revelation of the aspect of *Ein Sof* (which is above even the *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* before the *Tzimtzum*). And this is "for from You is their rest": since their rest (of Shabbos) is from the aspect of *yechidah*, therefore it is from You, literally from You.

5. The *maamar* there explains that the reason the *Anshei Knesses HaGedolah* instituted saying "You are One," etc., is that the *Knesses HaGedolah* is *Knesses Yisrael*, and the entire *hishtalshelus* (in the four aforementioned levels included in this text) was entrusted to the *Knesses HaGedolah* (*Knesses Yisrael*). And from the *Knesses HaGedolah* (*Knesses Yisrael*) it was entrusted to the entirety of the souls of Israel, souls in bodies, who are also able to say and draw down all these levels, beginning from the aspect of "You are One," which refers (as mentioned above) to the aspect of *Chesed* before the *Tzimtzum*, the aspect of the nature of the Good to do good, which is from the desire for *Chesed*. And this is "and you shall do them" (*ve-asitem etem*): read not "them" (*otam*) but "you" (*atem*), "as if you made Me" through compulsion (for the meaning of "made Me" in *Lashon HaKodesh* is a matter of compulsion, like a *get* that is coerced). This means that you compel Me, as it were, to be called by the name of *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* even in the aspect of *Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*. The power for this in the souls of Israel is because the root of the souls is in *Ein Sof* itself (which is above even the *Chesed-Gevurah-Tiferes* in *Ein Sof*), and even after they are clothed in bodies, this aspect shines in them in the aspect of *yechidah* within them (as mentioned above). Therefore, they have the power to perform (and in a manner of compulsion) all four aforementioned levels.

6. According to all the above, one can understand why the delivery of the *drush* "Atah Echad" was on Shabbos (Parshas Vayeitzei) before the *Mincha* prayer [even though generally, the delivery of *Chassidus* is at the time of *Rava D'Ravin*, as is apparent with our Rebbes, after the *Mincha* prayer], because the saying of "You are One," etc., is to effect the drawing down of the four aforementioned levels. And since all matters are drawn down through Torah, therefore, before the saying of "You are One" in the *Mincha* prayer, there was the drawing down of this matter in Torah. And since the drawing down of the four levels in the text of "You are One" is from the root of the souls in *Ein Sof* which is revealed in the aspect of *yechidah* as mentioned above, therefore, in Torah itself, there was the drawing down of this matter as it is in the inner dimension of Torah, and as the inner dimension of Torah is in a manner of "they shall be sustained" [Chabad *Chassidus*], the aspect of *yechidah* of Torah.

7. It can be said that this is also the connection of the delivery of this *drush* to the last Shabbos of the imprisonment, Erev *Yom HaGeulah*. For it is known that through the imprisonment, a greater elevation was achieved. This is similar to the teaching of our Sages: the olive, when it is crushed (specifically), gives its oil. And although the matter of "spreading your springs outward" existed even before the imprisonment (of the Mitteler Rebbe), nevertheless, through the imprisonment, it was increased, and it can be said that it was an incomparable increase. This is according to the precision of the language of our Sages that through the crushing it *gives* its oil (specifically "gives"), for whoever gives, gives with a generous eye. The magnitude of the elevation that was renewed specifically through the imprisonment is understood from what is explained in the *sicha* of the Rebbe Rayatz (regarding the imprisonment of the Alter Rebbe, from which it is also understood regarding the imprisonment of the Mitteler Rebbe), that the imprisonment was with his consent, for every *tzaddik* is a ruler and master over all his physical matters, and every event with him is with his consent. From the fact that he consented to the imprisonment, even though it was connected with suffering, etc., and with the prevention of spreading the teachings of *Chassidus* (while he sat in prison), it is understood from this the magnitude of the elevation of the spreading of the springs that was renewed specifically after his redemption, to the extent that for this, the entire imprisonment was worthwhile. This is the connection of the delivery of this *drush* to (Erev) *Yom HaGeulah*, for then a greater elevation was renewed in the revelation of the inner dimension of Torah [the aspect of *yechidah* of Torah], a revelation in understanding and comprehension, and the revelation is not only in *Chochmah* and *Binah* but also in the aspect of *Da’as*, for the root of *Da’as* is in the inner dimension of *Keter* [and as explained at length in the *Hemshech* *Eis Tzeischa* regarding the virtue of the matter of *Da’as*], the aspect of *yechidah*. This is "Your children will recognize and know for from You is their rest," for the revelation of the aspect of *yechidah* ("from You is their rest") is specifically in the matter of *Da’as*—"will recognize," etc., "and know."

8. This is also what Mashiach will teach—*Da’as* to all the people in the comprehension of the inner dimension of Torah—specifically *Da’as*. For the main matter that will be renewed in the days of Mashiach is [as the Rambam writes at the end and conclusion of his book] "for the earth will be full of the *Da’as* of Hashem," specifically *Da’as*. And this revelation will be in a manner of "as the waters cover the sea," meaning that it will be (in addition to *Da’as* and intellectual recognition, also) in tangible sight, like the waters of the sea that are seen in tangible sight. For it is explained in many places regarding the creatures in the *olam ha-mitkaseh* (the hidden world) that they are like fish in the sea, which are nullified to the water, to the extent that the existence of the fish is not seen at all, and what is seen are only the waters of the sea that cover them. From this it is understood that the meaning of "as the waters cover the sea" is that the revelation of *Elokus* will then be in tangible sight, like the waters of the sea (that cover the creatures within them) which are seen in tangible sight.

May it be the will that from the study of these matters in Torah, we will come soon to the fulfillment of the promise (of these matters) in actuality, that from within true rest and from within joy and goodness of heart, we will go toward the true and complete *Geulah* through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, when then "the earth will be full of the *Da’as* of Hashem as the waters cover the sea."

## With the help of Hashem. Shabbos Parshas Vayeitzei, 10 Kislev, 5746

**"He has redeemed my soul in peace from those who draw near to me, for there were many with me."** It is well known that beyond the simple meaning of this verse—that Dovid spoke regarding his own redemption, that Hashem redeemed him from the wars that came upon him—this verse also alludes to the redemption of several Tzaddikim, and certainly to the redemption of the Tzaddikim who recited this verse at the time they were redeemed. It is worth noting that in all three of the redemptions of our Rebbes—the redemption of the Alter Rebbe on 19 Kislev, the redemption of the Mitteler Rebbe on 10 Kislev, and the redemption of the Rebbe Rayatz on 12 Tammuz—the masters of the redemption recited this verse on the day they were redeemed. Regarding the Alter Rebbe’s redemption on 19 Kislev, it is known what the Alter Rebbe wrote in his Iggeres HaKodesh: "When I read in the Book of Tehillim the verse, 'He has redeemed my soul in peace,' etc., I emerged in peace from the 'Hashem of Peace.'" Regarding the Mitteler Rebbe’s redemption on 10 Kislev, it is known that our Rebbes would recite the daily portion of Tehillim as divided by the days of the month; since the verse "He has redeemed my soul" is in the monthly Tehillim portion for the tenth day of the month, he recited this verse on the day of his redemption (the tenth of Kislev). And regarding the redemption of the Rebbe Rayatz on 12 Tammuz, it is known that it was the custom of our Rebbes that at special times and situations, they would add the recitation of the Tehillim portion as divided by the days of the week (in addition to the monthly portion). Since the calendar placement of 12 Tammuz in the year 5687 (the year of the imprisonment and redemption) was on the third day of the week, and the verse "He has redeemed my soul" is in the weekly Tehillim portion for the third day, it is understood that the master of the redemption of 12 Tammuz recited this verse on the day of his redemption.

It is explained elsewhere that the connection of the 10 Kislev redemption to the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace" is (in one respect) even greater than the connection of the 19 Kislev and 12 Tammuz redemptions to this verse, for since this verse is in the Tehillim portion for the tenth day of the month, the connection of this verse to the redemption of 10 Kislev is emphasized every single year.

**b)** It is known how the master of the redemption explains in his Maamar (printed in his sefer *Shaarei Teshuvah*) that the emphasis in the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace" is (in addition to the matter of redemption itself) on the *manner* of the redemption—that the redemption is in peace, without any war whatsoever. He explains there that the true essence of peace is that one has no adversary at all. This is similar to how it was in the days of Shlomo, when all the nations were *batel* (nullified) to him. Seemingly, the intention is that as long as one has adversaries, even if they are not actually fighting him (because his awe and fear have fallen upon them, etc.), this is not the true essence of peace. For when there are adversaries, there is a possibility and a place for war, whereas the true essence of peace is (not only that there is no actual war, but) that there is (also) no possibility or place for war.

This is why the matter of peace existed specifically in the days of Shlomo—which is why he was called Shlomo, because there was peace (*shalom*) in his days—for all the nations *became batel* to Shlomo (due to the intensity of his greatness). When the adversary is nullified (at which point there is no place for him to fight), that is the true essence of peace. This is what Hashem said to King Dovid (when he wanted to build the Beis HaMikdash): "You shall not build a house for My name, for you are a man of war," even though at that time (when Dovid wanted to build the Beis HaMikdash) he was in a state of rest, and Hashem had given him rest from all his enemies round about. This is because Dovid’s rest came about because he had conquered all the wars, not because his enemies had become *batel*. Since adversaries still remained in his days (and there was a place for them to fight), this was not the true essence of rest and peace. This is the meaning of "He has redeemed my soul in peace"—the matter of redemption in peace is that the adversary is nullified, as it was in the days of Shlomo.

The Maamar continues that the true essence of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" is that those who were previously enemies and adversaries become lovers. This matter is even higher than the peace that existed in the days of Shlomo, for the peace in the days of Shlomo was that everyone became *batel* due to the revelation of light (but they were not transformed into holiness), whereas the true essence of peace (that even the adversary is transformed into holiness) will be in the future, as it is written: "For then I will turn to the peoples a clear language, etc., to serve Him with one shoulder." And as for the fact that *Dovid* said "He has redeemed my soul *in peace*" (even though in his days the adversaries were not nullified, and certainly not transformed into holiness, as this did not exist even in the days of Shlomo and will only be in the future), it is explained in the Maamar of the master of the redemption that this refers to the soul of Dovid as it will be in the future.

According to what is known regarding the interpretation of the Sages' statement that "one is judged by his own words" (and similarly regarding the interpretation of the Mishnah’s language of "judgment and accounting"), when a person issues a verdict for another person, he is thereby issuing a verdict for himself. Thus, the verdict of the Mitteler Rebbe in his Maamar *Podeh B'Shalom Nafshi*—that the meaning of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" is that the adversary is nullified (like the peace in the days of Shlomo), and furthermore, that the adversary is transformed into a lover (like the peace that will be in the future)—is a verdict also regarding the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" in his own redemption, the redemption of 10 Kislev. (From this, it is also understood regarding the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" in the redemption of 19 Kislev and the redemption of 12 Tammuz, that the peace in these redemptions is that the adversary is nullified, and furthermore, that he is transformed into a lover.)

**c)** The simple interpretation of the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace" is that Dovid said this regarding his own redemption as it exists in this world, that *this* redemption was in peace. One must understand the connection and relevance of the interpretation in the Maamar—that Dovid’s statement, "He has redeemed my soul in peace," refers to the soul of Dovid as it will be in the future—with the simple interpretation that Dovid’s redemption now was a redemption in peace. The explanation for this can be said based on what the Rambam writes: that the section of Bilaam speaks of the two Mashiachs—the first Mashiach, who is Dovid, and the final Mashiach, who will arise from his descendants. It can be said that the reason Dovid is called the first Mashiach is because all the matters that will be brought about by King Mashiach (the final Mashiach) had their beginning through Dovid, the first Mashiach. However, their completion and perfection will be through the final Mashiach who arises from the descendants of Dovid. Based on this, we can explain the connection between the two interpretations of the verse "He has redeemed my soul in peace": that in their inner essence, Dovid’s redemption now was also a redemption in peace—meaning that the adversaries were *nullified* (and not merely that they did not fight him), and furthermore, that they were transformed into lovers. However, in Dovid, this was in an internal and hidden manner, and in the future, the inner essence of the redemption of Dovid’s soul will be revealed, which is a redemption in peace.

Based on this, the greatness of the elevation of the 10 Kislev redemption is understood even more, for the fact that it is described as a redemption in peace (without the added explanation that it is a preparation for the redemption in peace of the future, etc.) proves that the matter of peace within it [that the adversary is nullified, and furthermore, that he is transformed into a lover] is (also) in a revealed state.

**d)** It is known that Shabbos and Yom Tov require preparation on the day before them—Erev Shabbos and Erev Yom Tov. Regarding our matter, the festival of the redemption of 10 Kislev—its "Erev," the day that serves as the preparation for 10 Kislev, is the ninth of Kislev, the birthday and Yahrtzeit of the master of the redemption. Even though the redemption of 10 Kislev occurred in the year 5687, a year before his passing, nevertheless, since "merit is brought about through a meritorious day," it can be said that the fact that the ninth of Kislev merited to be the Yahrtzeit of the Mitteler Rebbe is because even before that, it was connected to this. Based on this, it can be said that the greatness of the elevation of the day of redemption [10 Kislev], where the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" is similar to the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" that will be in the future, is because its "Erev" is the Yahrtzeit of the master of the redemption.

**e)** This can be understood by prefacing what the master of the redemption writes in his Maamar *Lehavin Inyan Hilula d'Rashbi*: that the reason the day of the passing of the Rashbi is called a "Hilula" (wedding celebration) is because through the Rashbi, there was the revelation of the inner dimension of the Torah. He explains this by prefacing the Sages' statement: "This world, from which we depart, is like a wedding celebration." Through the descent of the souls below into this world—*ha'alma*—they undergo the process of *azlinan*, "we are walking/moving." Souls, as they are above, are called "standing," as it is written: "As Hashem, the G-d of Israel, lives, before whom I have stood." And in the Zohar it is stated: "Every single soul was *standing* in its form before the Holy King." Although souls, as they are above, are always in a state of *ratzo v'shov* (running and returning), through which they ascend from level to level, nevertheless, since all their ascents are within limitation—for even the highest levels (where the souls ascend) are according to the "measuring line"—they are therefore called "standing." Through the descent of the souls below, they become "walking," for the ascent of the souls through their descent below is in a state of *Ein Sof* and without limitation. This is the meaning of "this world, from which we depart"—the souls, as they are from their own perspective, are standing, and through their descent into this world, they become walking, *azlinan*.

The reason that it is specifically through this world that the matter of *azlinan* is achieved is because this world is like a wedding celebration. The matter is that the soul and the body are like a man and a woman. Just as with a man and a woman in the literal sense, for the union of male and female (who are two opposites) to occur, it is through the drawing down of the power of the *Ein Sof* that is above both of them—and this power that is drawn into them is revealed in the matter of procreation [for the matter of bringing forth generation after generation without limit is not from the nature of created beings, who are limited, but from the power of the *Ein Sof* that is above the nature of created beings, which is drawn down through marriage]—so too is it with the soul and the body, which are like a man and a woman. For the connection and union of the soul and body, which are two opposites, to be possible, it is through the drawing down of the power of the *Ein Sof*. And the drawing down of this power is revealed in the matter of procreation—"the offspring of the righteous are good deeds"—for the ascent achieved through Torah and Mitzvos is an ascent in a state of *Ein Sof* and without limitation. This is "this world, from which we depart, is like a wedding celebration"—the reason that specifically from it (this world) comes *azlinan* (we become walking) is because this world is like a wedding celebration, where the connection and union of the spiritual soul with the physical body (this world) is like the union of a bridegroom and bride (Hilula, a wedding), through which the power of the *Ein Sof* is drawn down.

**f)** It is known that just as the descent of the soul below is a descent for the sake of an ascent, so too is it with Torah and Mitzvos; the intention in the descent of Torah and Mitzvos is for the sake of an ascent. For through their descent below and their enclothement in physical things, the essential power of Torah and Mitzvos is drawn into them. Based on this, it can be said that the matter of Hilula in the performance of Torah and Mitzvos in this world specifically also applies to the Torah and Mitzvos themselves. In addition to the matter of Hilula in the person engaged in Torah and Mitzvos—the drawing down of the power of the *Ein Sof* through the connection and union of soul and body (as in section e)—there is also the matter of Hilula in the Torah and Mitzvos themselves. For Torah and Mitzvos are the wisdom and will of Hashem, which are above the world, and their connection with the matters of this world (the physical things in which the Torah and Mitzvos are enclothed) is a connection of two opposites. This connection is through the drawing down of the essential power of Torah and Mitzvos—similar to the union of bridegroom and bride, through the drawing down of the power of the *Ein Sof* that is above both of them and connects them.

**g)** The Maamar continues with another interpretation of "this world, from which we depart": that at the time of a person’s passing, when the soul departs from the body and from this world—*azlinan minei*—then the matter of *azlinan* is achieved within it, that it becomes in a state of "walking." According to the explanation above (section e), the soul becoming "walking" is while it is below in this world. And the interpretation of *azlinan minei* is "because of it and by its cause"—that the fact that the soul comes to the state of *azlinan*, that it becomes "walking," is *minei*, from the elevation of this world, which is like a wedding celebration. According to the second interpretation, the matter of *azlinan* that is achieved in the soul (that it becomes "walking") is at the time of *azlinan minei*, when it goes and departs from this world. The core of the explanation in the Maamar is that the Mitzvos, as they are below, are in a state of measurement and limitation. And the Torah of below is also in a state of measurement and limitation, as it is written: "Its measure is longer than the earth"—meaning that there is a measure to the Torah, but the measure is longer than the earth. Therefore, the "walking" (the matter of being without limit) that is achieved in the soul through its engagement in Torah and Mitzvos in this world is in a state of concealment. Also, the "walking" of the soul through the Mitzvah of procreation, even though in the matter of procreation there is a revelation of the power of the *Ein Sof*, nevertheless, since the revelation of the power of the *Ein Sof* in this is regarding a limited matter (the birth of children, albeit that the birth is generation after generation without limit), therefore, even through the performance of this Mitzvah, the matter of "walking" is in a state of concealment. After the soul fulfills its mission in this world and the time of *azlinan minei* arrives, when it goes (and departs) from the limitations of this world, then the inner essence of the Torah and Mitzvos in which it engaged in this world is revealed to it—that the inner essence of Torah and Mitzvos is without limit—and then the matter of *azlinan* (walking) is achieved within it in a revealed state as well. This is why the day of the passing of Tzaddikim who engaged in the inner dimension of the Torah is called a "Hilula" day (like the Hilula of the Rashbi, and similarly regarding the day of passing of all Tzaddikim who engaged in the inner dimension of the Torah), for through the study and engagement in the inner dimension of the Torah, the inner essence and the "without limit"—the matter of Hilula and walking—is revealed on the day of passing.

**h)** The master of the redemption explains in the aforementioned Maamar that in a Hilula (the union of male and female) there are three matters (in general): The union of male and female above. The matter of male and female also exists in souls as they are above before their descent below; there are souls that are in the category of male and souls that are in the category of female. The union of the souls that are in the category of male with the souls that are in the category of female is called a Hilula. And the union of male and female below. In this itself, there are two matters: the union of male and female in the literal sense, and the union of the soul with the body [which are like a man and a woman]. The third matter in a Hilula is the union of the soul (male) with the body (female) that will be at the resurrection of the dead. This Hilula is the highest level, for then there will be a revelation of the Essence.

The revelation that will be at the resurrection of the dead is (as explained in many places, and also in the aforementioned Maamar) that then the drawing down of the Essence that was achieved through the performance of Mitzvos in this world will be revealed. This is similar to what was explained above (section g) regarding the matter of the Hilula at the passing of Tzaddikim who engaged in the inner dimension of the Torah, which is the revelation of the inner essence and the "without limit" of the Torah and Mitzvos in which they engaged in this world. However, now, in order to receive the revelation, it is through the departure of the soul from the body, whereas in the future, this revelation will be even in the physical body (and the revelation itself will be higher, for then there will be a revelation of the Essence, as mentioned above).

It can be said that one of the reasons the matter of the third Hilula is explained in the discourse *Lehavin Inyan Hilula d'Rashbi* is to hint that the merit of the Tzaddikim whose primary engagement is to reveal the inner dimension of the Torah is in two matters: that their day of passing is called a "Hilula" day because then the inner essence and the "without limit" in the Torah and Mitzvos are revealed, as explained at length above. And also, that through their work to reveal the inner dimension of the Torah, the "without limit" of the Torah and Mitzvos is revealed [to them, and through them it is revealed somewhat also to all those connected to them] even in the soul as it is enclothed in the physical body, and also in the physical body itself, *similar* to how it will be at the resurrection of the dead. It should be added that the revelation of both matters, including the matter of the Hilula of passing, is in the soul as it is enclothed in the body. However, initially, the revelation of the "without limit" to the person is through his being in a state of *bitul* (nullification), where he exits his own existence—similar to the matter of passing—and through this, the "without limit" is subsequently revealed also *within the existence* of the person—similar to the revelation that will be at the resurrection of the dead.

**i)** From the above, it is understood that in the aforementioned "walking" (ascent in a state of *Ein Sof* and without limit) that is achieved through the performance of Torah and Mitzvos in this world, there are three levels. In souls that engage only in the revealed dimension of the Torah, the "walking" is in a state of concealment. For from the perspective of the external dimension (the revealed dimension of the Torah), the Torah and Mitzvos are in a state of measurement and limitation. And the revelation of the "without limit" that is drawn down through the performance of Torah and Mitzvos in this world will be primarily in the future. In souls that engage in the inner dimension of the Torah, the revelation of the "without limit" of the Torah and Mitzvos (the matter of "walking") is also at the time of their passing, and similarly while they are below, through the movement of *bitul* and exiting their own existence. Through this, they subsequently arrive at a higher level, where the revelation of the "without limit" of the Torah and Mitzvos is also within their own existence.

It can be said that it is similar regarding the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace," that it has (similar to) these three levels. The matter is that the true essence of "He has redeemed my soul in peace," that the adversary is nullified completely, as explained above (section b) from the Maamar of the master of the redemption, is through the revelation of the *Yechidah*. For the level of *Chayah*, since there is an "other side" (*sitra achra*) opposing it, its revelation does not negate and nullify completely the foreign desires of the animal soul; it is only that the desire for G-dliness (of the level of *Chayah*) overcomes the desires of the animal soul. For there to be a nullification of the desires of the animal soul, it is through the revelation of the *Yechidah*. Since the level of the *Yechidah* has no "other side" opposing it—"in its shadow, they have no power"—therefore, through the revelation of the *Yechidah*, the "other side" is nullified automatically.

This is why the true essence of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" will be with the coming of Mashiach specifically. For just as in a person, the nullification of the "other side" within him is through the revelation of the *Yechidah* specifically, so too is it in the world at large—that specifically through Mashiach, who is the *Yechidah* of the entire *Seder Hishtalshelus*, there will be a complete nullification of the *Sitra Achra*. However, since all the revelations of the future depend on our deeds and our work now, the matter of the nullification of the *Sitra Achra* through the revelation of the general *Yechidah* that will be in the future is also through the person revealing the *Yechidah* within himself, through which the foreign desires of his animal soul are nullified. In particular, when through the revelation of his *Yechidah* he also brings about (to a degree) the nullification of the *Sitra Achra* in others. Just as King Dovid, through his work, brought about that even Amasa said to him, "Peace, peace to you and peace to your helpers," and that even the men of Avshalom prayed for Dovid’s victory.

Since now all matters are in a state of limitation, therefore, the matter of *mesiras nefesh* that comes from the revelation of the *Yechidah* is also regarding a limited matter. [Similar to what was explained above (section g) regarding the revelation of the power of the *Ein Sof* in procreation, that the revelation of the *Ein Sof* is in a limited matter.] From this it is understood that although the revelation of the general *Yechidah* of the future is through the revelation of the *Yechidah* now, nevertheless, the revelation of the *Yechidah* now is incomparable to the revelation of the general *Yechidah* of the future. Similarly, regarding "He has redeemed my soul in peace" that Dovid experienced: since his redemption in peace was only regarding several people (Amasa, the men of Avshalom, etc.) and not in the world at large (for in the world at large there were adversaries), it is therefore incomparable to the redemption in peace of the future, which will have no limitations. The drawing down of the redemption in peace of the future (without limit) through the redemption in peace of Dovid (which is regarding limited matters) is similar to the drawing down of the matter of "walking" and the "without limit" through the performance of Torah and Mitzvos in this world by those who engage only in the revealed dimension of the Torah. And the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" in the redemption of 10 Kislev [and similarly in the redemption of 19 Kislev and 12 Tammuz], since the redemption is regarding the inner dimension of the Torah—the *Yechidah* of the Torah—therefore, the matter of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" in this redemption has no measurements or limitations, and it is regarding all matters.

This is why the ninth of Kislev, the Yahrtzeit day, is the "Erev" of the day of redemption, for through the fact that the revelation of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" occurred after the preceding work of the Hilula of the master of the redemption—the matter of *bitul* (which is similar to passing)—it drew down this revelation (the revelation of "He has redeemed my soul in peace" that is above measurement and limitation) even *within the existence* of all those connected to him. Therefore, in order to receive this revelation, there is no need for a preparation of *bitul* and exiting one's existence; the matter depends only on the will and the ability of each person (even before he has exited his own state) to redeem himself in the manner of "He has redeemed my soul *in peace*," peace in the ultimate perfection, and through this, to redeem the entire world as well, in the true and complete redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, speedily and truly in our days, and in our very midst.

## 14 Kislev, 5739

"Who created joy and gladness, bridegroom and bride, mirth, song, delight and rejoicing, love and brotherhood, peace and friendship." My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, cites in his maamar titled *Zeh* (found in the *Kuntres Drushei Chassuna 5689*), the statement of the Zohar in *Parshas Terumah*, which explains each of the seven *brachos* in detail. Regarding the seventh *bracha*—which includes all the *brachos*—it says: "The seventh is that which sustains everything, and from this seventh everything is blessed; it is certainly the totality of the 'ten utterances,' because it includes both above and below, and therefore it includes these ten types of joy, etc." The maamar explains that the "totality of the ten utterances" includes the Ten Commandments (*ila*—above) and the ten utterances of creation (*tata*—below). Since this *bracha* is the totality of the ten utterances, it contains ten expressions of joy (aside from the words "bridegroom and bride"), which represent the joy in each *sefirah* specifically (for it is precisely through this that the *sefiros* are in their state of perfection, as is understood from what is explained in many places regarding the verse "the mother of children is joyful"). The Zohar continues there (quoted in the maamar) regarding the seventh *bracha*: "Fortunate are the Jewish people, for they have merited to act below in the likeness of above." This means that this *bracha* is also praise and thanksgiving to Hashem for the fact that the Jewish people, through their *avodah*, clarify and refine the lower realm (*tata*) so that it becomes in the likeness of the upper realm (*ila*), and through this, they draw down the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* into the upper worlds as well.

2) The explanation of this matter (and also the connection of "meriting to act below in the likeness of above" to the subject of bridegroom and bride) is that all matters below are derived from their source above—in the language of the *Tanya*, they "are drawn down from them." This is also true of the bridegroom and bride below: they are drawn down from the bridegroom and bride above, Hashem and the *Knesses Yisrael*. As explained in the *Midrashim* of our Sages, the bridegroom and bride mentioned in *Shir HaShirim* refer to Hashem and the *Knesses Yisrael*. As our Sages said, "On the day of His wedding"—this refers to the Giving of the Torah, for the Giving of the Torah is the marriage of Hashem and the *Knesses Yisrael*. It can be said that this is the concept of "a good heart is a constant feast," for every single day there is the event of the Giving of the Torah, as we say in the *bracha* on the Torah (which we recite every day), "Giver of the Torah"—in the present tense. To the extent that every day, "whoever reads and studies, Hashem reads and studies opposite him." The Alter Rebbe explains that this fact—that Hashem reads and studies opposite him—is exactly as it was at the time of the Giving of the Torah the first time. Therefore, they said (regarding the practical *halacha*), "Just as there it was with awe, fear, trembling, and shuddering, so too here it must be with awe, fear, trembling, and shuddering." Especially according to the explanation that these four expressions (awe, fear, trembling, and shuddering) correspond to the four letters of the Name *Havayah*, for at the Giving of the Torah there was the revelation of the Name *Havayah* to every single Jew, and this revelation exists also in the engagement with Torah now.

Now, through engagement with Torah, one also acts upon the world to make it a vessel for *Elokus*. For through the fact that the Torah "traveled and descended, etc.," until it was clothed in physical things so that a person could grasp it in his thought, speech, and action, through this, one draws down into the world as well. It is known that the ten utterances with which the world was created correspond to the Ten Commandments, as it is in the Zohar on the verse "ten, ten, the palm in the holy shekel." Therefore, through engagement with Torah (the Ten Commandments), one acts upon the ten utterances so that they, too, will be in the "holy shekel."

It can be said that the fact that the ten utterances and the Ten Commandments are weighed against one another also means that each acts upon the other. Just as the Ten Commandments act (as mentioned above) upon the ten utterances, and as the statement says, "He looked into the Torah and created the world"—meaning that the creation of the world is through the ten utterances as they are in the Torah, which then descended into the state of "commonplace words" to bring the world into being—so too is it the other way around: the person's *avodah* in the matters of the world, which was created through the ten utterances as "commonplace words," acts upon the Ten Commandments (which is why there is an interpretation in the Zohar on the verse "ten, ten, etc.," that the ten utterances are higher than the Ten Commandments). It can be said that this is according to what is explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh* that the clarification of *halacha* in the Torah is specifically through a soul in a body, and therefore the heavenly beings come to hear *chiddushei Torah* from those below (as our Sages said on the verse "the companions listen to your voice").
