The fact that the goblet was subsequently found is because after the *ha'alaas MAN* was already awakened within them, the *himshech* came in a revealed way. Through this, they were elevated even more than their elevation when the goblet was hidden. This is why, after the goblet was found, Judah said, "G-d has found the iniquity of your servants," because after he was elevated to a higher level, even the most subtle matter was considered an iniquity to him. Then, "Judah approached him," and his *ha'alaas MAN* [and that of all the *shevatim*] was in perfection.

7) When Judah wanted to awaken Joseph to bestow upon him the *himshech* of *MAD*, he said to him, "Let your servant, I pray you, speak a word in my lord’s ears... for you are like Pharaoh." The explanation for this is that since the *himshech* from *Ze'er Anpin* to *Malchus* is a descent, Judah explained to Joseph that, on the contrary, through this there would be an ascent in *Ze'er Anpin*, that "you will be like Pharaoh." Joseph, as he is from his own perspective, starts from *Chochma*, which is the beginning of revelation, and by bestowing upon "your servant"—*Malchus*—he will be like Pharaoh, *Keter*, and within *Keter* itself—the inner dimension of *Keter*.

This is the connection between Judah’s approach to Joseph and *semichas geulah l’tefillah*, because in Judah’s approach to Joseph, the virtue of *Malchus* relative to *Ze'er Anpin* is emphasized: that through *Ze'er Anpin* bestowing upon *Malchus*, an elevation is added to *Ze'er Anpin*. This is similar to "great is *talmud* because it leads to *maaseh*," for through this, a greatness is created in *talmud*. Based on this, one can also understand the connection between "Judah approached him" and the *Haftorah* of Parshas Vayigash, the content of which is that in the time to come, Judah will be higher than Joseph, because the elevation that occurs in *Ze'er Anpin* through its influence upon *Malchus* is a precursor to that which (in the time to come) will reveal the virtue of *Malchus* itself—"And My servant David shall be king over them... and David My servant shall be their prince forever"—with the coming of *Mashiach Tzidkeinu* very soon.

## Yehudah, You Shall Your Brothers Praise

**Yehudah, you shall your brothers praise**. The Alter Rebbe explains in Torah Or at the beginning of our Parsha that Yehudah is named for the expression, "This time I will thank [odeh] Hashem". This represents the level of *hodiyah* (gratitude/acknowledgment) within the collective souls of Yisrael. For this *hodiyah* to be revealed, it must come through "your brothers"—Reuven, Shimon, and Levi (the brothers of Yehudah who were born before him). This is the meaning of "your brothers shall praise you": your brothers (Reuven, Shimon, and Levi) are the ones who cause and bring about the level of *hodiyah* of Yehudah (so that it may be in a state of revelation). He explains there that Reuven and Shimon correspond to the two paragraphs of Krias Shema, and Levi corresponds to *Emes V’Yatziv* (which follows Krias Shema). Through this, one subsequently arrives at the Shemoneh Esrei, which is the level of *hodiyah* and *bitul* of Yehudah.

2) The concept is (as explained in Torah Or there) that Reuven is named for "for He has seen," and Shimon for "for He has heard." Their roles in *avodas Hashem* (for every individual in Yisrael) are as follows: The level of Reuven is that his service to his Creator is not merely through intellectual grasp, but through a level of "seeing." This means his verification of Elokus is with the greatest possible certainty, like a person who sees something and for whom it is impossible to have any doubt, because he himself saw the thing. Similarly, in *avodas Hashem*, his contemplation [especially regarding the matter of *hashgacha pratis*] is in a manner of *histaklus* (gazing) where the matter is verified for him as if he were seeing it with actual sensory vision. From this "seeing," he arrives at the level of love for Hashem, as it is written (regarding the naming of Reuven), "For He has seen... for now my husband will love me." This is the subject of the first paragraph of Krias Shema: "And you shall love Hashem your G-d..."

The level of Shimon (named for "for He has heard") is that his service is in the level of hearing and intellectual grasp alone, not in the level of seeing. Since he is still in a state of distance [like a person who hears something being told to him, but has not seen it himself], his service is therefore along the line of *yirah* (fear/awe). This is the subject of the second paragraph of Krias Shema: "And it shall be if you will surely hear... take heed to yourselves lest your heart be seduced..." Since the matter of Elokus for him is only in the level of hearing ("if you will surely hear"), his service must therefore be along the line of *yirah* ("take heed to yourselves").

The level of Levi, named for "my husband will be joined [yillaveh] to me," is what we say after Krias Shema: *Emes V’Yatziv*... "this matter is upon us." "This matter" refers to the Torah, because through the Torah, "my husband will be joined to me" is accomplished (as will be explained).

3) To explain: these three levels (Reuven, Shimon, and Levi) are the three pillars upon which the world stands [including this "small world," which is man, for "you are called man"]: Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*. The level of Reuven (love) is the pillar of *gemilus chasadim*. For when one has love of Hashem, one also has love of Yisrael [since love of Hashem and love of Yisrael are one and the same], and therefore his service is along the line of *gemilus chasadim*, because love is the cause and the inwardness of *chesed*, an *hemshech* (drawing down) from above to below. The level of Shimon (fear) is the pillar of *avodah*, the matter of *tefillah*, the left line [and within *tefillah* itself, it is the second paragraph of Krias Shema, as mentioned above], an *ala'ah* (ascent) from below to above. The level of Levi, named for "my husband will be joined to me," is the pillar of Torah, because through *Oraisa* (Torah), Yisrael and the *Kudsha Brich Hu* are one. This is the meaning of "my husband will be joined to me," for through the Torah, a connection is made with "my husband"—this is the *Kudsha Brich Hu*—to the point that Yisrael and the *Kudsha Brich Hu* are one. This is why the level of Levi is *Emes V’Yatziv*... "this matter," which refers (as mentioned above) to the Torah.

4) This is "Yehudah, you shall your brothers praise": the service of the levels of "your brothers"—Reuven, Shimon, and Levi, the two paragraphs of Krias Shema and *Emes V’Yatziv*—is a preface and preparation for the level of Yehudah, the service of the Shemoneh Esrei, *hodiyah* and *bitul*, like a servant before his master. The *bitul* of the *tefillah* of Shemoneh Esrei is a *bitul* in the ultimate sense, higher even than the *bitul* of Krias Shema. This is also understood from the differences in the laws (regarding interruptions, etc.) between Krias Shema and Shemoneh Esrei. This matter [that through the service of the levels of Reuven, Shimon, and Levi, one subsequently arrives at the level of Yehudah (the fourth son)] is similar to the well-known concept regarding the three crowns [the crown of Torah, the crown of Kehunah, and the crown of Malchus, which correspond to the three pillars: Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*] and the crown of a good name which rises above them [which is higher than them all]. In order to reach the level of the crown of a good name, it is through the three crowns; it rises *above them*.

5) Now, the fact that the level of Yehudah requires the preface of the service of the levels of Reuven, Shimon, and Levi is so that the *hodiyah* of the level of Yehudah may be in a state of revelation. But in concealment and in inwardness, this *hodiyah* is constant. As explained in Torah Or, the *yud* of Yehudah indicates the present and the constant, like "thus would Iyov do all the days," meaning that the *hodiyah* within every individual in Yisrael is constant. That which is written, "This time I will thank Hashem" (for not all times are equal), is only regarding the revelation, due to the concealment and hiding of the body and the *nefesh habahamit* (and especially due to the concealment and hiding of the *galus*). But the *nefesh elokis* within every individual in Yisrael [which is his true essence] is always "faithful with Him," and "I will thank Hashem" is constant for him; no change is possible in this, G-d forbid. The preface of the service of the levels of Reuven, Shimon, and Levi is to awaken the *hodiyah* that is in the inwardness of the heart so that it may be in a state of revelation, until its effect is brought from potential into actual reality in thought, speech, and action.

6) The verse continues, "Your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies," for through the revelation of the *hodiyah* of the level of Yehudah, "your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies" is accomplished in a spiritual sense (as explained in Torah Or) and also in a physical sense, for "a verse does not depart from its literal meaning." After "your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies," it is said, "A lion's whelp is Yehudah... the scepter shall not depart from Yehudah," and it is possible to say that this hints that "your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies" is even before the *Geulah*. For "a lion's whelp... the scepter shall not depart from Yehudah" refers to Dovid and Shlomo, from whose seed will come *Melech HaMoshiach* of whom it is said, "A scepter shall arise from Yisrael" [at which time "My servant Dovid shall be king over them," and "Dovid My servant shall be a prince to them forever," as was written in the *Haftorah* of this past Shabbos, "from whom all days are blessed"]. Before that, at the conclusion of the time of *galus*, "your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies" will occur, meaning that all the enemies of Yisrael [for all of Yisrael are called by the name Yehudah] will be struck with fear and dread through the revelation of the *hodiyah* within the collective souls of Yisrael, until it comes into actual practice in thought, speech, and action—"If you walk in My statutes and keep My commandments..."—and through this will come (as the verses continue in Parshas Bechukosai) "and your enemies shall fall before you."

7) It is possible to say that this is also the connection between "Yehudah, you shall your brothers praise; your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies" and "Yaakov lived [vayechi] in the land of Mitzrayim." Even when Yisrael is in *galus* (for all *galuyos* and kingdoms are called by the name Mitzrayim), they are in a state of *vayechi* ("and he lived") through the study of Torah—the Torah of life—and the fulfillment of its commandments, of which it is said, "and he shall live by them" [and as the Alter Rebbe explained to his grandson, the Tzemach Tzedek, regarding the verse "Yaakov lived in the land of Mitzrayim," that through the mission of *Yehudah*—"he sent Yehudah before him... to instruct him before him to Goshen to prepare for him a house of study, etc."—there was a level of "living" even in Mitzrayim]. Through this is accomplished ("Yehudah, you shall your brothers praise") "your hand shall be on the nape of your enemies," as our Sages said, when the voice is the voice of Yaakov in the synagogues and houses of study, the hands of Esav do not prevail. Furthermore, the nations of the world themselves assist Yisrael in all physical matters so that they may study Torah and fulfill commandments in a state of expansion, in a manner of "increasing and going forward, increasing and adding light." And this light pushes away the darkness of *galus* and brings the *Geulah* through *Moshiach Tzidkeinu*, who will come and redeem us and lead us upright to our land, speedily in our days, truly.

## Shabbos Parshas Shemos, 19 Teves 5748

"Those who come shall take root, Yaakov shall blossom and bud..." The Tzemach Tzedek, in his maamar on this verse, cites the Targum Yonasan ben Uziel’s interpretation that this refers to the Redemption. The Targum explains "those who come" as "those who will be gathered from their exiles and return to their land" [as "those who come" refers to the arrival in the land that will take place in the future Redemption, as it is written, "And Hashem your G-d will bring you to the land"]. At that time, "Yaakov shall take root," and "those of the house of Yaakov shall be born." The maamar explains that "taking root" (*yishresh*) refers to concealment (like the roots of a tree, which are hidden in the ground), while "birth" (*yivaledun*) refers to revelation (for before birth, while in the mother’s womb, the child is in concealment, and upon birth, he comes into revelation). Thus, the Targum’s translation of *yishresh* as "they shall be born" means that the root of the soul (*yishresh Yaakov*) will be brought into revelation (*yivaledun*).

The maamar continues with another interpretation of "Yaakov shall take root," referring to the fulfillment of mitzvos. Just as a root in its literal sense must be in the ground, so too with the soul (Yaakov): its rooting is through the fulfillment of practical mitzvos. The connection between these two interpretations of *yishresh Yaakov* (the revelation of the soul’s root and the fulfillment of mitzvos) is that the revelation of the soul’s root, which will occur in the future, is achieved through the fulfillment of mitzvos now. The concept is that there are many levels to the root of the soul. The primary root of the soul is its source in the light that transcends the *Seder Hishtalshelus*, and this light is drawn down through the fulfillment of mitzvos. The drawing down of light through Torah study is from the level of Chochma, the beginning of *Hishtalshelus*, whereas the drawing down of the light that transcends *Hishtalshelus* is through the performance of mitzvos. This is what our Sages meant when they said, "Anyone whose deeds exceed his wisdom... is like a tree whose branches are few but whose roots are many," because the drawing down of the soul’s root is through the performance of mitzvos. This is the connection between the two interpretations: through the fulfillment of mitzvos now (the second interpretation), the root of the soul is drawn down, and the revelation of this drawing will be in the future, "those of the house of Yaakov shall be born," when the root of the soul will shine in revelation (the first interpretation).

Now, according to the known precision in the maamarim of our Rebbes, including the order of the subjects, one must understand: since the revelation of the soul’s root in the future comes after and through the fulfillment of mitzvos now, why does the maamar first present the interpretation that *yishresh Yaakov* refers to the revelation of the soul’s root in the future, and *only afterwards* present the interpretation that it refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos now? Furthermore, why does the first interpretation (that it refers to the Redemption) also explain the word "those who come," while the second interpretation (that it refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos now) does not explain the word "those who come"?

2. Regarding the interpretation that *yishresh Yaakov* refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos, there are two aspects: that the rooting of Yaakov is in the fulfillment of mitzvos (as above), and that the rooting of the mitzvos is in Yaakov. As the Alter Rebbe (the *baal hahilula* of 24 Teves) writes in *Torah Or* on this verse, just as literal planting must be in the ground, so too with the planting of mitzvos ("Light is sown for the righteous"), it must be specifically in Yisrael, of whom it is written, "For you shall be a land of desire." This is "Yaakov shall take root, he shall blossom and bud," where *yishresh Yaakov* refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos now—that the mitzvos are sown and rooted in Yaakov—and through this, the growth, "he shall blossom and bud," will occur in the future.

The *Torah Or* explains there that the meaning of "those who come" (*habaim*) shall take root is that in order for there to be *yishresh Yaakov*—the rooting of Torah and mitzvos in Yisrael—it is through "those who come," the coming to Egypt. As is known, the exile in Egypt was a preparation for the giving of the Torah. It continues there that just as the Egyptian exile was a preparation for the giving of the Torah, so too this final exile is a preparation for receiving the inner dimension of the Torah that will be revealed in the future. It can be said that the reason *Torah Or* adds this point (that the final exile is a preparation for the inner dimension of the Torah) is to harmonize the connection of "those who come" to "Yaakov shall take root," as the verse implies that the rooting happens within the exile itself. Regarding the Egyptian exile, "those who come" is only a preface to *yishresh Yaakov*. Through the Egyptian exile—the "iron furnace"—Yisrael were refined to become a "land of desire," and through this, their fulfillment of Torah and mitzvos after the exodus and after the giving of the Torah was in the manner of *yishresh Yaakov*. Therefore, it adds that the final exile is a preparation for the revelation of the future, according to which "those who come" refers also to the exiles following Egypt up to this final exile. According to this interpretation, "those who come" and "Yaakov shall take root" are one matter: the rooting of mitzvos in Yisrael (through which the growth will come in the future) is primarily through the mitzvos that Yisrael fulfill during the time of exile. As it is written in *Tanya*, all the revelations of the future depend on our deeds and our *avodah* during the duration of the exile.

It may be said that the reason the aforementioned *maamar* of the Tzemach Tzedek does not cite this interpretation—that "those who come" refers to the descent into exile—is that the fact that the revelations of the future depend on our deeds and *avodah* applies to our deeds and *avodah* in all times, both during the time of the Beis Hamikdash and during the time of exile, as each has its own advantage (as will be explained). *Yishresh Yaakov* (according to the explanation in this *maamar*) refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos in all times.

3. The matter is that regarding the advantage of fulfilling mitzvos over studying Torah, there are two points. The drawing down achieved through fulfilling mitzvos is more exalted than that achieved through Torah study (as in section 1), and fulfilling mitzvos possesses the advantage of *is'hapcha* (transforming darkness into light), because the refinement of the body, the *nefesh habehamis*, and one’s portion in the world is (primarily) through the performance of mitzvos. Among the differences between these two matters is that the drawing down through mitzvos was greater during the time of the Beis Hamikdash than during the time of exile. For the drawing down through the performance of mitzvos is (primarily) when the action is done with *bitul*, and since the matter of *bitul* is primarily during the time of the Beis Hamikdash [for in addition to the fact that in exile it is impossible to have the *bitul* of prostration (internal *bitul*) which comes specifically from seeing Elokus, as we say, "We cannot go up and appear and prostrate ourselves before You"—since we cannot go up and appear, we cannot prostrate ourselves to be in a state of internal *bitul*—the external *bitul* (the *bitul* of bowing), which comes from the apprehension of Elokus (which exists, generally, even in exile), is also not as it should be, because even the apprehension of Elokus in exile is not a true apprehension], therefore, the drawing down through the fulfillment of mitzvos during the time of the Beis Hamikdash was a more exalted drawing down. The primary drawing down through mitzvos will be in the future, for then the fulfillment of mitzvos will be in ultimate perfection, "according to the will of Your desire." The matter of *is'hapcha* (transforming darkness into light) through the fulfillment of mitzvos is primarily during the time of exile. For the darkness of the body, the *nefesh habehamis*, and the world as they are in exile is a greater darkness than their darkness as they are in themselves, and through the fulfillment of mitzvos in exile, through which the refinement of the body, etc., as they are in exile is accomplished, this darkness too is transformed into light.

4. In the future, there will be the revelation of both aforementioned matters in the fulfillment of mitzvos: the revelation of the drawing down through the mitzvah itself, and the revelation of the drawing down through the refinement and *is'hapcha* accomplished by the mitzvah. It can be said that the reason *Tanya* states that the revelations of the future depend on our deeds and *avodah* in the time of exile is that *Tanya* there discusses the elevation of *is'hapcha* (transforming darkness into light), the "advantage of light that comes from darkness." The revelation of this light that will be in the future (the advantage of light that comes from darkness) depends (primarily) on our deeds and *avodah* during the time of exile, while the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is within the mitzvos themselves depends (primarily) on our deeds and *avodah* during the time of the Beis Hamikdash.

It may be said that the reason the revelation of the mitzvos themselves also depends on our deeds and *avodah* now—even though there will be fulfillment of mitzvos in the future, and on the contrary, the fulfillment of mitzvos then will be in ultimate perfection (and consequently, the drawing down through the mitzvos then will also be in ultimate perfection)—is that for the perfection of the drawing down of the mitzvos in the future to occur, it requires the precedence of the drawing down of the mitzvos as it is now. [Note the mitzvos of the Patriarchs, which were a preparation for the mitzvos after the giving of the Torah]. It may be said that this matter is also primarily through the *avodah* during the time of the Beis Hamikdash. For during the time of the Beis Hamikdash, man and the world were on a more exalted level than during the time of exile, and therefore, in the fulfillment of mitzvos during the time of the Beis Hamikdash [where the person who performed the mitzvah, as well as the physical object with which the mitzvah was performed, were on an exalted level], there was a perfection *similar* to the perfection of the fulfillment of mitzvos that will be in the future. Thus, the fact that the mitzvos of now are a preparation for the mitzvos of the future is primarily through the mitzvos of the time of the Beis Hamikdash. [It may be said that this is also one of the reasons why, during the time of exile, many matters were added to the mitzvos (hiddurim, customs, etc.), and also in Torah, and not only specific matters but also general matters—the Mishnah was arranged by Rabbeinu Hakadosh during the time of exile (though at that time there was not the *difficulty* of exile), and certainly the Beraisa and Gemara which were arranged during the time of exile—so that there would be perfection also in the *avodah* of Torah and mitzvos during the time of exile, and through this, also through the *avodah* of the time of exile, one will arrive at the true perfection of Torah and mitzvos of the future.]

5. The difference between the two aforementioned matters in the fulfillment of mitzvos (the drawing down through the mitzvah and the refinement accomplished through it) is also in the connection of the *avodah* now with the revelation that will come from it in the future. Regarding the drawing down through mitzvos, since the perfection of the drawing down is that it will be in revelation, the very nature of the (avodah and) drawing down now is that its revelation will be in the future. Regarding the refinement through mitzvos, since the nature of the *avodah* is the refinement of darkness—meaning that in this *avodah* it is felt that there is darkness that must be refined—the *avodah* now and the revelation of the future that will come through it ("and the spirit of impurity I will *remove* from the earth") are two separate matters.

According to this, one can understand why the aforementioned *maamar* does not bring the interpretation that "those who come" refers to the descent into exile, and also the order of the two interpretations in the *maamar*. For from the fact that the fulfillment of mitzvos now is called in the verse by the name "rooting," *whose nature* (planting) is to bring forth growth, it is proven that the matter of the fulfillment of mitzvos in this verse is the drawing down through the mitzvah, not the refinement accomplished through it. Therefore, it does not bring here the interpretation that "those who come" refers to the descent into exile, because the advantage of the *avodah* during the time of exile is regarding the refinement through the mitzvah, whereas the drawing down through the mitzvah is more in the *avodah* of the time of the Beis Hamikdash (as in sections 3-4). This is also why the interpretation that *yishresh Yaakov* refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos now comes after and in continuation of the interpretation that *yishresh Yaakov* refers to the revelation of the soul’s root that will be in the future, because the drawing down through the fulfillment of mitzvos now (in the verse *yishresh Yaakov*) is the drawing down of the soul’s root (as in section 1), and the nature of the drawing down is that it will be in revelation in the future, "they shall be born."

6. One must understand that according to the above explanation, the connection of the fulfillment of mitzvos now (the second interpretation) to the Redemption (the first interpretation) is in the matter of *yishresh Yaakov*, that the nature of drawing down the root now is that the drawing down will be in revelation. From the fact that the *maamar* says (at its beginning, even before citing the Targum Yonasan) that the Targum interprets the verse regarding the Redemption, it is understood that this is the primary interpretation. From this it is implied that the fact that the interpretation that *yishresh Yaakov* refers to the fulfillment of mitzvos now comes after and in continuation of the Targum’s interpretation is because the fulfillment of mitzvos now comes in continuation of the general matter of the Redemption.

It may be said that the reason it precedes in the *maamar* the interpretation that it refers to the Redemption is because all matters are drawn from the Torah—"He looked into the Torah and created the world"—and by the Torah preceding the matter of the Redemption, it is drawn down in the world as well that the Redemption will be sooner. To add: when a Jew (and certainly a Nasi) thinks about a good matter, his thought causes it to be so in actuality. As the Tzemach Tzedek said, which was publicized by the Rebbe Rayatz: "Think good and it will be good." The Rebbe Rayatz related that the Tzemach Tzedek said this to one of the Chassidim, and through the Chassid’s thought, it became good in actuality. It may be said that the reason the Rebbe Rayatz added this story is because through it happening in actuality, it is drawn down also in the physical. Similar to the known concept that the prophets also performed actions (physically) in accordance with their prophecies, because through this the connection of the prophecy with the (physical) world is created. It may be said that when thought comes into speech, and certainly into action, it is even more effective. And all the more so in our case, where in addition to the Tzemach Tzedek’s thought (to precede the interpretation that "those who come" shall take root refers to the Redemption) coming into writing (action), it is also a part of Torah.

7. May it be the will that through speaking about these matters, all of this will be hastened and accelerated even more, especially through the *avodah* of spreading Judaism in general and spreading the wellsprings in particular, which is a near preparation for the fulfillment of the prophecy, "The earth will be full of the knowledge of Hashem as the waters cover the sea," the conclusion and seal of the Rambam’s book (the *baal hahilula* of 20 Teves), which is also connected to the beginning of his book, "to know that there is a First Being." This is also connected to the *Tanya Kadisha*, the book of the Alter Rebbe (the *baal hahilula* of 24 Teves), in which the matters of the knowledge of Hashem are explained. May it be the will that the merit of the *baalei hahilula* who gathered and collected in their aforementioned books all the matters of Torah—the *Mishneh Torah* which gathers all the laws of the revealed Torah, and the *Likutei Amarim (Tanya)* which gathers all the matters of the inner dimension of the Torah, and also the books *Torah Or* and *Likutei Torah* in which the primary *maamarim* of the Alter Rebbe were collected as they were arranged by the Tzemach Tzedek—will hasten and accelerate even more the fulfillment of the prophecy in the *Haftorah*, "And you shall be gathered one by one, O children of Yisrael," by Hashem Himself, that very soon He will gather us all, and we will all go together to the Holy Land (as it will be in expansion, "Hashem your G-d will expand your border") and there itself—to Yerushalayim and the Beis Hamikdash, which will be built speedily in our days, truly.

## Parshas Shemos, 23 Teves, Mevarchim HaChodesh Shevat, 5713

"You who dwell in the gardens, the companions listen for your voice; let me hear it. Flee, my Beloved, and be like a gazelle or a young hart upon the mountains of spices." The Rebbe (Rashab) n.e. explains in his maamar on this verse, which was said on the 24th of Teves (the yahrtzeit of the Alter Rebbe), that "You who dwell in the gardens" refers to the revelation of Gan Eden. "Gardens" (plural) refers to the Lower Gan Eden and the Upper Gan Eden, which are in the worlds of Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah (the Lower Gan Eden in Asiyah and the Upper Gan Eden in Beriah). "You who dwell in the gardens" refers to Malchus of Atzilus, which constricts itself to dwell within the gardens. "Flee, my Beloved... upon the mountains of spices" refers to the revelation of the time to come, which is far more elevated—incomparably so—than the revelation of Gan Eden. For the revelation of Gan Eden is the light that fills all worlds (*Memaleh*), while the revelation of the time to come is the light that encompasses all worlds (*Sovev*). This is the meaning of "Flee... upon the mountains of spices" ("flee" specifically), for the ascent from the revelation of Gan Eden to the revelation of the time to come is by way of "fleeing" and leaping, outside the order of progression. Nevertheless, in order to receive the revelation of the *Sovev* light of the time to come, it must be specifically through the precedence of the revelation of the *Memaleh* light that is in Gan Eden. This is "You who dwell in the gardens (and afterwards) flee, my Beloved... upon the mountains of spices"—first there must be the revelation of "You who dwell in the gardens," and specifically through this, one reaches (by way of fleeing and leaping) the revelation of the "mountains of spices."

b) The explanation of this matter: The difference between the *Memaleh* light and the *Sovev* light is that the *Sovev* light is a simple light, whereas the *Memaleh* light comes in a differentiated manner—the light of Chochma, the light of Chesed, etc. It is explained in the aforementioned maamar that from the fact that the *Memaleh* light comes in a differentiated manner—even though the differentiation in practice is through its enclothement in the *keilim* (vessels)—it is necessary to say that even in the light itself (before its enclothement in the *keilim*) there is a connection to differentiation. For it is explained in *Pardes* that the differentiation created in the light through its enclothement in the *keilim* concerns only its function (the manner in which it acts upon the created beings; when it is enclothed in the vessel of Chesed, it performs the action of Chesed, and similarly with all the *Sefiros*), but the light itself remains in its simplicity. Based on this, one might say that the light in its own right (before its enclothement in the *keilim*) is entirely simple (having no connection to differentiation at all), like the analogy of sunlight (cited in *Pardes*), which, although it is a simple light in its own right (for light is akin to the luminary and attached to the luminary), nevertheless, when it shines through a white, red, or green glass, it appears (and acts) according to the nature of the vessel. However, on the face of it, this explanation is insufficient. For it is known that the *Sovev* light, even when it shines within the *keilim*, the vessel does not cause any change in it, even if it only appears according to the manner of the vessel [as is also brought in the aforementioned maamar, that the light of Atik, even as it is drawn into Ze'er Anpin, is in a state of simplicity]. From this it is proven that the fact that in the *Memaleh* light there is created (by the *keilim*) a configuration and differentiation (at least regarding its function) is because this light (even before its enclothement in the *keilim*) is in the category of differentiation.

c) The matter is that the light of the *Kav* (the *Memaleh* light is generally the light of the *Kav*) is drawn down through the *Tzimtzum*, which is an extension by way of leaping. The concept of the *Tzimtzum* (the first *Tzimtzum*) is that the light that was there initially was completely hidden, leaving a void and an empty space. Since the subsequent extension comes after the complete concealment (interruption) of the light, the subsequent extension is by way of leaping. [And even though at the time of the *Tzimtzum* a *Reshimu* remains, as explained in many places, and as is also brought in the aforementioned maamar that "the void is not literal" but rather a *Reshimu* remains, the concept of the *Reshimu* is a hidden power, and as is known, the *Reshimu* is the power of limitation within the *Ein Sof*, the root of the *keilim*. But regarding the matter of light and revelation, the *Tzimtzum* is a complete removal and concealment, and the extension of light after the *Tzimtzum* is an extension by way of leaping.] Therefore, even the attachment of the light of the *Kav* is an attachment that comes through an interruption (and is not in the category of true attachment), and consequently, it is not in the category of light and revelation to such a degree. This is like the analogy of the vitality of the hair, which is not comparable to the vitality of the limbs; it is a highly constricted vitality, to the extent that when one cuts the hair, one feels no pain, because the manner of its extension from the brain is through the interruption of the skull. Similarly, it is in the analogy above: the *Sovev* light, since its extension from its source and its attachment to its source is not through an interruption [for even though the *Sovev* light is also a light drawn down after the *Tzimtzum*, it is known that the effect of the *Tzimtzum* on the *Sovev* light is (primarily) not regarding the light itself, but rather that it should not shine in revelation (inwardly), but only in the category of *Makif* and *Sovev*. For in the *Memaleh* light, the *Tzimtzum* is regarding the light itself, for through the *Tzimtzum* a light is drawn (and created) that did not exist previously. Whereas the effect of the *Tzimtzum* on the *Sovev* light is (as above) that it should be in the category of *Makif* and *Sovev*, but the light itself is not a light that was created afterwards, but rather it is the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* from before the *Tzimtzum*, a revelation not through an interruption], therefore it is true light and revelation (like the vitality of the limbs), and is not in the category of configuration and differentiation. Whereas the *Memaleh* light (the light of the *Kav*), whose manner of extension from the *Ohr Ein Sof* and its attachment to the *Ohr Ein Sof* is through the interruption of the *Tzimtzum*, is not in the category of revelation to such a degree (but rather like the vitality of the hair), and therefore it is in the category of differentiation (meaning, that through its enclothement in the *keilim*, the light will appear according to the manner of the vessel).

d) This is why the ascent from the revelation of Gan Eden to the revelation of the time to come is by way of "fleeing" (as explained above (at the beginning of the maamar) regarding the precision of the language "Flee... upon the mountains of spices," "flee" specifically). Since the difference between the *Memaleh* light (the revelation of Gan Eden) and the *Sovev* light (the revelation of the time to come) is (not only that the *Memaleh* light comes in differentiation through the *keilim*, but also) in the manner of their extension from their source—for the *Memaleh* light is drawn through the interruption of the *Tzimtzum*, whereas the *Sovev* light is not—it is understood that the difference between them is an incomparable difference. Therefore, the ascent from the revelation of Gan Eden (*Memaleh* light) to the revelation of the time to come (*Sovev* light) is by way of "fleeing." Just as the extension of the light of the *Kav* from the *Ohr Ein Sof* from before the *Tzimtzum* is by way of leaping, so too the ascent from the *Memaleh* light (the light of the *Kav*) to the *Sovev* light (which is the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* from before the *Tzimtzum*) is by way of "fleeing," outside the order of progression.

e) **However**, although the revelation of Gan Eden is incomparable to the revelation of the time to come, nevertheless, the revelation of Gan Eden is a prerequisite through which (specifically) one reaches the revelation of the time to come (as mentioned at the beginning of the maamar). The matter is that before the *Yud-Gimmel Middos HaRachamim* it is written "Havayah, Havayah," with a *pasiq* (a pause) between them. It is known that the first *Havayah* is the *Havayah* of above, the Name *Havayah* that is in the *Ohr Ein Sof* from before the *Tzimtzum*, and the second *Havayah* is the *Havayah* of below, the Name *Havayah* that is in the light of the *Kav*, and the *pasiq* between them is the interruption of the *Tzimtzum* between the *Ohr Ein Sof* and the light of the *Kav*. This is also what is meant in the first verse of *Krias Shema* : "Havayah, Elokeinu, Havayah," for *Elokeinu* is the *Tzimtzum* between the *Havayah* of above (*Ohr Ein Sof* from before the *Tzimtzum*) and the *Havayah* of below (the light of the *Kav*). The maamar explains that the mention of *Elokeinu* (between the two Names of *Havayah*)—for *Elokeinu* (unlike *Elokim*), while it indicates the attribute of *Din* (judgment), has an inclination toward *Chesed* and *Rachamim* —is because through *Elokeinu* specifically is the connection so that the revelation and the *shefa* (bounty) will be drawn from the *Havayah* of above to the *Havayah* of below. One can say the explanation for this is along the lines of what is known regarding the *keilim*: although if the light of the *Kav* had remained initially, it would have been impossible for the *keilim* to come into being, nevertheless, after the *keilim* came into being, they are able to receive the revelation of the light of the *Kav*. For since their coming into being was through the *Tzimtzum* by way of removal specifically, they were created in such a manner that not only are they not nullified by the revelation of the light of the *Kav*, but furthermore, the revelation of the light is through the *keilim* specifically [for in the *Tzimtzum* by way of removal specifically, the *Etzem* (Essence) is revealed that is above the category of revelation (lights) and concealment (*keilim*), and therefore through the *Tzimtzum* by way of removal, the connection of *keilim* and lights is made]. Similarly, it is also regarding the light of the *Kav*, that through its extension from the *Ohr Ein Sof* being through the *Tzimtzum* by way of removal, through this it is made in such a way that it will not be nullified by the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* (the Infinite), but furthermore, through it specifically will be the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof*.

f) The maamar continues that according to what is explained in other places that *Elokeinu*—our G-d—is the Name *Havayah* that is in every single Jew, "a portion of *Havayah* is His people", it is understood even more that *Elokeinu* connects the two Names. For through the *avodah* of the Jew with the four letters of *Havayah* in the soul, the connection of the two Names is made, so that in the *Havayah* of below, the revelation of the *Havayah* of above shines. It explains there that the *Yud* of the Name *Havayah* in the soul is the surrender of his essence—that he completely surrenders himself to *Elokus* and does not take his own intellect into account. Not only does he not take it into account when his intellect dictates otherwise (including the fact that he does not care if people say his conduct is contrary to intellect), but furthermore, that he surrenders himself to *Elokus* not because his intellect dictates so (as explained in other places that the intellect itself dictates that there are matters above intellect), but rather that he surrenders himself to *Elokus* without any calculations. The concept of the first *Hei* is the contemplation of how all the revelations in the worlds, even the revelation of the *Sovev* and certainly the revelation of the *Memaleh*, are only a mere illumination drawn down through many, many *Tzimtzumim*. Through this, he feels extremely constricted, and especially when he contemplates his own personal standing and situation, how he is distanced from *Elokus* [for the reason for this, that he is in such a state, is because the light and revelation that shines in the worlds in general is only a constricted illumination, for the further the illumination is drawn downward, the more it is constricted, which is the reason he came to such a state], from which he feels even more constricted, until he finds no place at all. It can be said that in order for this understanding and comprehension to truly take root in him, there must first be the *bitul* of the surrender of his essence—the concept of the *Yud*, a mere point, and this also acts upon the intellect so that it will align with the point, "a point in the palace". The concept of the *Vav* of the Name *Havayah* in the soul is the engagement in Torah. For after the contemplation (the *avodah* of the first *Hei*) that the entirety of the revelations in the worlds are only a constricted illumination, from which he becomes extremely constricted (as above), then "they cried out to *Havayah* in their distress", that he cries out (and prays) to *Havayah* that there should be the revelation of the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the *Tzimtzum*, and this brings him to the engagement in Torah, through which the revelation of the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof* shines for him. For even though "the Torah was issued from Chochma", it is known the precision of the *Baal HaHillula* (of the 24th of Teves), that from this very expression ("issued from Chochma," "issued" specifically) it is understood that the root of the Torah is the Supreme Will, etc., which is above Chochma, and only afterwards does it come into Chochma, and from there it is issued in a state of revelation. The concept of the final *Hei* of the Name *Havayah* in the soul is the performance of the *mitzvos*, which comes after the *avodah* of the *Vav*, Torah, for in order to fulfill the *mitzvos* as they should be, one must know the action that one must do.

g) And here, although the *avodah* of the performance of the *mitzvos* is the final *Hei*, which is below the engagement in Torah which is the *Vav*, from this itself it is understood that there is a superiority in *mitzvos* over Torah. As explained at length in the aforementioned maamar, that the Torah, although its root is the Supreme Will, etc., which is above Chochma (as above), the will of the Torah is enclothed in Chochma, from which it is proven that this will (even before its enclothement in Chochma) is a will that is compounded with something. Whereas the will of the *mitzvos* is a will that is not compounded with anything. For the will of the *mitzvos* is in the actual performance specifically, for the actual performance (in physicality) is not in the category of value at all, and consequently, it is not possible to say that the will is enclothed in the action (for the concept of enclothement is only in something that has value), and therefore the will in the action is a will that is not enclothed and not compounded with anything. And through the performance of the *mitzvos*, an elevation is added even to the understanding and comprehension of Torah, as it is written "Teach me good taste and knowledge, for I have believed in Your *mitzvos*," for through the fulfillment of the *mitzvos* (in Your *mitzvos*), and especially when the fulfillment of the *mitzvos* is not because of the taste and the *kavanah* (intention) but only because of simple faith ("I have believed"), one reaches the "good taste and knowledge" that is in Torah.

h) This is "You who dwell in the gardens... flee, my Beloved... upon the mountains of spices." For it is known that the revelation in Gan Eden is (primarily) that which is revealed to the souls—the inwardness and the *Elokus* of the Torah that they learned below. Whereas the revelation of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is drawn down through the *mitzvos*, this will be revealed primarily in the time to come, for "there is no reward for *mitzvos* in this world", at which time there will be the revelation of the *Sovev* light, and even higher, there will be the revelation of the Essence. This is why the revelations of the time to come are called "mountains of spices," meaning "mountains in the heavens" (*shamayim*). *Shamayim* encompasses all worlds, and "mountains in the heavens" (that even in the heavens they are mountains, prominent) is the concept of the revelation of the Essence that is above even the *Sovev*. And it says regarding this "Flee... upon the mountains of spices," for the revelation of the *Memaleh* (in Gan Eden) relative to the revelation of the *Sovev* (*shamayim*), and certainly relative to the revelation of the Essence ("mountains of spices"), is incomparable at all [for relative to the Essence, even the *Sovev* is incomparable. And as it is written "You have done many things, O Havayah my G-d, Your wonders and Your thoughts toward us; there is none to compare to You," etc. "Many" are the totality of all the revelations of the *Sovev* and *Memaleh*, which are called "many" along the lines of what is said in the *Zohar* "What is 'many'? Great, exalted, and mighty." And regarding this it says "You have done many things," that all the revelations, although they are "many" (exalted and mighty), are like an action relative to Him, blessed be He. And it continues in the verse "Your wonders and Your thoughts," etc. "Your wonders" are the revelations of the *Sovev* (a wonder, which is above the order of progression), and "Your thoughts" are the revelations of the *Memaleh*, and it says that the entire revelation of "Your wonders and Your thoughts" is only "toward us," but relative to Him, blessed be He, they are incomparable—"there is none to compare to You." And their extension, "I would declare and speak" (declaration and speech is the concept of extension), from the essence of *Ohr Ein Sof*, is through a multitude of *Tzimtzumim*, "they are too numerous to count"]. And since the *Sovev* and certainly the Essence are above revelation, therefore their revelation in the time to come is through the precedence of the *Memaleh* light (which shines in Gan Eden), whose concept (of the *Memaleh* light) is revelation, and therefore through it specifically, even the matters that in their own right are in the category of concealment are drawn down into revelation.

i) One can add to this that it is along the lines of what is known in the interpretation of "The companions listen for your voice". "Companions" are the ministering angels, and they "listen for your voice"—to the voice of the Torah and prayer of the Jew. For since the voice and speech of a person is physical, therefore, in order for the Torah and prayer of the person to ascend, it is through the angels refining them, for "He embraces them and kisses them". And through the angels elevating the letters of the Torah and prayer of the Jew (the aforementioned interpretation of "listen for your voice"), as a reward for this, they "listen for your voice," that they receive from the letters of the Torah and prayer of the Jew, which are far more elevated than the angels, even to an incomparable elevation. Similarly, it is also regarding the matter of the revelations of the time to come: although they are incomparably elevated relative to the revelations of Gan Eden, nevertheless, since the revelations of the time to come are the light that is drawn down through the performance of *mitzvos* in physical things in this physical and material world, therefore they need to be refined by ascending to Gan Eden, for Gan Eden is refined to the ultimate degree (as is known the reason for this, that Adam HaRishon was driven out of Gan Eden, for Gan Eden cannot tolerate the existence of sin). But now, even after they are refined in Gan Eden, the essence of the *mitzvah* does not shine in revelation, but only an illumination, the *Memaleh* light. And through this (since the concept of the *Memaleh* light is revelation), there will be in the time to come the revelation of the essence of the *mitzvah*, the revelation of the *Sovev*, and even higher—"mountains of spices"—the revelation of the simplicity of the Essence.

## 24 Teves, 3rd Day of Parshas Va’era, 5732

"And Elokim spoke to Moshe and said to him, 'I am Hashem,'" and it concludes, "Therefore, say to the Bnei Yisrael, 'I am Hashem.'" There are well-known points of precision regarding this from the Baal HaHillula: What is the novelty in "I am Hashem" that begins the speech, as the verse continues to explain? The novelty is that even though "I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchak, and to Yaakov," it was only as Kel Shakkai, but "My name Hashem I did not make known to them."

One must also understand the general content of the matter: "I appeared to Avraham... and My name Hashem I did not make known to them." On one hand, this indicates the superiority and novelty of Moshe relative to the Patriarchs, that specifically in the days of Moshe, the name Hashem was revealed, whereas the Patriarchs did not have this knowledge and grasp of the name Hashem. Yet, at the same time, the Midrash states regarding this verse and subject (cited in Rashi on the Torah) that the Ribono shel Olam said, "Woe for those who are lost and are not found; I appeared to Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov... and they did not question My attributes, whereas you said..." Seemingly, these are two contradictory matters.

Similarly, one must understand the statement of our Sages (cited in the *D’h* "HaChodesh Hazeh Lachem," 5708) regarding Moshe, that "all seventh ones are beloved," which contains a paradox. When it says that the seventh ones are beloved, it implies a greater level of affection than all those that preceded them. At the same time, the fact that we say Moshe’s belovedness is due to his being the seventh implies (as explained in the aforementioned *D’h*) that his belovedness is because he is the seventh to the first. This is especially so according to the Midrash (cited in the *Maamar* there) that when Moshe sought a matter belonging to the "great ones," the Ribono shel Olam told him, "Do not stand in the place of the great ones," where "the place of the great ones" refers to Avraham.

This can be understood by first citing the statement of the Baal HaHillula in *Torah Or*, Parshas Shemos, and also in one of the *bichlach* (booklets) released recently, *D’h* "Lehavin HaBeur B’marzal" on the verse, "Sixty are the queens, and eighty are the concubines, and maidens without number. One is my dove, my perfect one; she is the only one to her mother, she is the choice one to her who bore her." "Sixty are the queens" refers to the sixty tractates; "eighty are the concubines" refers to the *baraisos*; "and maidens without number" refers to the *halachos*, which are the statements of the Amoraim. It concludes that all of this is not comparable to "one is my dove."

To understand this, and also to understand the statement of our Sages that "great is study, for it leads to action"—for seemingly we find many *halachos* that have no relevance to action, especially according to what is explained in *Kuntres Acharon* that there are matters that "never were and never will be," yet there are *halachos* and debate regarding these matters—it is explained there by first citing the well-known principle that the purpose of creation is to make for Him, *yisbarach*, a dwelling place in the lower worlds. This dwelling place in the lower worlds is created through the "sixty queens," "eighty concubines," and "maidens without number"—in general, through Torah. Through engagement in Torah—what is pure, kosher, and permitted—that thing is clarified. Then, through the *avodah* of Yisrael, that thing is refined and elevated until it becomes a dwelling place for Him, *yisbarach*, in the lower worlds.

Just as a dwelling requires fine vessels—"do good"—one must also remove undesirable matters, which is the concept of "turn from evil." This is the Torah as it is in the line of *Gevurah*, where the ruling is the opposite of pure, kosher, and permitted, because it is not fit to be a vessel for *Elokus*—a dwelling for Him, *yisbarach*, in the lower worlds. This is the concept of "sixty are the queens," the sixty tractates, for specifically in the *Torah Sheba’al Peh* is the clarification of the will of the Creator to know the matters of "turn from evil" and "do good." The entirety of *Torah Sheba’al Peh* is divided into six, which are the six orders of the *Mishnah*, and each one is comprised of ten, from which the "sixty queens" are made. It is called "Malchus" because in the *Mishnah*, *Elokus* is revealed, like a queen whom the King visits openly and in a permanent manner. It can be said that this is the connection of the *Mishnah* to the "house"—the time when the Beis Hamikdash existed. Even though the arrangement and completion of the *Mishnah* was by Rabbeinu HaKadosh (which was after the time of the Beis Hamikdash), our Sages said, "And it remained in the days of Rebbe," meaning the days of Rebbe were a continuation of the time when the Beis Hamikdash existed.

"Eighty are the concubines" are the *baraisos*. *Baraisa* comes from the word *bar* (outside), because at that time there was the matter of *galus*—we were exiled from our land, which is the concept of *bar* and outside. Thus, the revelation of the will and the revelation of *Elokus* that was in the Torah at that time was not as clear, and therefore it is called a "concubine." Afterward, it descended even lower: "maidens without number" are the *halachos*, which are the statements of the Amoraim. This includes everything that a veteran student will eventually innovate, especially the *Talmud Bavli*, of which it is said, "He has placed me in darkness"—this is the Torah of the time of *galus* specifically, and even until the final *galus*. They are called "maidens" (*alamot*) from the word *he’elem* (concealment) and *hester* (hiding), for the concealment and hiding in them is greater than in the *baraisos*. But nevertheless, specifically in them is the matter of "without number," through which the ultimate clarification is achieved, to the point of "without number." This is a prelude to the time of "the number of the Bnei Yisrael shall be as the sand of the sea, which cannot be measured."

Although through this (the matter of Torah) a dwelling is made for Him, *yisbarach*, there must be the level of "one is my dove," in order to connect the Torah with the Giver of the Torah. This is through His choosing us from all the nations and giving us His Torah, at which point "Hashem is with him," that the *halacha* follows him everywhere. For this, there must be the *avodah* of *tefillah*, through which the level of "one" is created, receiving from the One. The beginning of the *avodah* of *tefillah* is the level of "my dove," as it is written, "Your eyes are doves." This is the *hisbonenus* (contemplation) and the looking within the *Krias Shema* that precedes the *tefillah* (*Shemoneh Esrei*). Even before that, in the *brachos* of *Krias Shema* and *Pesukei D’zimra*, one contemplates and looks—this is the concept of "your eyes are doves," etc., until it becomes "Shema Yisrael Hashem Elokeinu Hashem Echad." *Shema* is also a term of gathering and collecting, for one gathers all his matters and becomes a receiver from "Hashem Elokeinu," which is the level of "one," until he reaches the level of "with all your might," which is the matter of *mesiras nefesh*.

Through the *avodah* of "one is my dove," it becomes "my perfect one" (*tamati*), from the word *temimus* (wholeness) and *shleimus* (perfection), which effects perfection—fulfilling and completing the will and desire that the Ribono shel Olam desired, to have for Himself, *yisbarach*, a dwelling place in the lower worlds. This is through the Torah, through which one effects in the world all the matters mentioned above. There is also a second interpretation of *tamati*: *te’omati* (my twin), that one becomes a partner with the Ribono shel Olam in the work of creation (and all the explanations explained in the *drushim* on the verse in *Torah Or* and *Likutei Torah* on *Shir HaShirim* by the Baal HaHillula). It concludes, "She is the only one to her mother, she is the choice one to her who bore her," as explained in the aforementioned *drushim*.

This is what is meant by "And Elokim spoke to Moshe and said to him, 'I am Hashem.'" The *galus* of Mitzrayim was like an "iron furnace." In order for the world to be able to receive the Torah and the study that leads to action—the matter of *mitzvos*—it was necessary to precede it with "and they embittered their lives and they enslaved the Bnei Yisrael with hard labor," which is the clarification and refinement performed by the "iron furnace," which prepared the world to receive the Torah. This is unlike the time of the Patriarchs before the *galus* of Mitzrayim, when the world was not prepared, etc. Through the Torah, the truth of Hashem is drawn into the world, for the Torah is called "the Torah of Hashem," and it acts upon the "In the beginning Elokim created" so that it will be in the ultimate perfection, similar to what was explained above regarding "my perfect one," that the supernal will that was in the creation of the world is completed—"In the beginning" (*Bereishis*) for the sake of the Torah, which is called "beginning" (*reishis*).

However, as stated above, although the drawing down of the dwelling place in the lower worlds is through the "sixty queens," "eighty concubines," and "maidens without number"—the matter of Torah—there must be before this the matter of *tefillah*, the level of "one is my dove," through which the connection of Yisrael to the *Kudsha Brich Hu* is made. Afterward, and through this, Yisrael acts upon the Torah, which connects to the *Kudsha Brich Hu*, and then it is drawn from above to below. As is known, one of the explanations of the statement of our Sages, "Three bonds are bound one to the other: Yisrael, the Torah, and the *Kudsha Brich Hu*." Seemingly, in a bond of three things, there are only two connections! Rather, Yisrael also connects to the *Kudsha Brich Hu* directly, as it is in a circle. The connection is not only from above to below—that the *Kudsha Brich Hu* is above, below that is the Torah, and below that is Yisrael—but Yisrael themselves also connect to the *Kudsha Brich Hu*, and through this they draw from the *Kudsha Brich Hu* into the Torah, similar to what is explained that Dovid would connect the Torah to the *Kudsha Brich Hu*.

Just as through the level of "one is my dove," which is the matter of *tefillah*, one effects the connection of the Torah to the *Kudsha Brich Hu*, so too is it regarding the matter of "I am Hashem." This is the matter of the Torah, which is the truth of His essence—"You take Me" through the Torah, for the inwardness of *Abba* is the inwardness of *Atik*, which is the essence and being. Before this, there must be "I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchak, and to Yaakov," which is the matter of *tefillah*, for "the Patriarchs instituted the *tefillos*." Specifically, Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov are the three *tefillos* of *Shacharis*, *Mincha*, and *Maariv*, which was the preparation for the *avodah* of the children, for "the deeds of the Patriarchs are a sign for the children," that there must be the two matters of Torah and *tefillah*.

This is "And Elokim spoke," etc. When Yisrael needed to leave the *galus* of Mitzrayim, the level of *Gevurah* was still in revelation, which is the matter of the name *Elokim*. Like Pharaoh, who said, "I do not know Hashem," for he does not belong to the name Hashem, because his matter and root is that he draws from the 48 final combinations of the name *Elokim*. Therefore, even among Yisrael, to distinguish, who were then in the *galus* of Mitzrayim, it was also in such a manner. But afterward, "And Elokim spoke to Moshe and said to him, 'I am Hashem,'" for the time had arrived for the level of "I am Hashem" to be revealed. This is through the preface of "I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchak, and to Yaakov," which is the matter of *tefillah* as mentioned above, the level of "one is my dove." From the perspective of this level, it is not appropriate to question His attributes, as our Sages said, "With all your might—with every measure that He measures for you." Afterward, and through this, the level of "I am Hashem" is drawn down, and it is drawn down through Moshe, as it says, "Therefore *say* to the Bnei Yisrael, 'I am Hashem.'" Moshe received the Torah from Sinai and transmitted it, etc.—the giving of the Torah to Yisrael, through which the name Hashem will be in revelation. The Ten Commandments will be drawn into the ten utterances with which the world was created, a redemption from the concealment and the straits (*Mitzrayim*), and in all four expressions of redemption—"I will take out," "I will rescue," "I will redeem," and "I will take"—until "I will bring you to a land that is good" (the opposite of concealment) "and wide" (the opposite of *Mitzrayim*), speedily and truly in our days, through Moshiach Tzidkeinu.

## B's"d. Shabbos Parshas Va'eira, Mevarchim HaChodesh Shevat, 5735

### B's"d. Shabbos Parshas Va'eira, Mevarchim HaChodesh Shevat, 5735

"I appeared to Avraham, to Yitzchak, and to Yaakov as Kel Shaddai, but by My name Havayah I was not known to them". Rashi explains: "I was not recognized by them in My true attribute." The Admur HaMaharash notes in his maamar on this topic that all the names of the Ribono shel Olam are true, yet it states that specifically the name Havayah is the "true attribute." Furthermore, we must understand the other side of Rashi’s statement: he calls the name Havayah a "true *attribute* (middah)," yet the name Havayah is the *Shem HaEtzem* (Essential Name), which transcends measurement and limitation. How can it be called a "middah"—a term derived from measurement and limitation? The maamar explains that even the name Havayah is only an *oar* (illumination), and therefore it is called a *middah*. At the conclusion of the maamar, it states that there is a level that transcends the revelation of Havayah as it is now. This implies that the revelation of Havayah that will occur in the future will transcend *middah* (measurement) entirely. This can be connected to what the Alter Rebbe (the *baal hahilula* of 24 Teves) said: the reason the name Havayah was not known to the Avos is that the Torah had not yet been given, and the revelation of the name Havayah comes through the giving of the Torah. Based on this, we can say that since in the future there will be a revelation of the *pnimiyus* (inner dimension) of the Torah—which transcends measurement—the revelation of Havayah in the future will likewise transcend *middah*.

2) The fact that "I appeared to Avraham as Kel Shaddai" refers to what the Ribono shel Olam said to Avraham (at the mitzvah of milah): "I am Kel Shaddai; walk before Me and be whole". The revelation Avraham received at the mitzvah of milah was certainly an extremely lofty one. We must understand (as the maamar emphasizes): the name Shaddai already existed at the creation of the world, as the Gemara states: "What is the meaning of the verse 'I am Kel Shaddai'? It is I who said to My world, 'Dai' (enough)." The firmament was expanding until the Ribono shel Olam rebuked it and said, "Dai." What, then, was the novelty in the Ribono shel Olam saying to Avraham, "I am Kel Shaddai"? Seemingly, one could say that the creation of the world occurs through the concealment and hiding of the Creator from the created, and by saying "I am Kel Shaddai" to Avraham, He illuminated this name in a state of revelation. However, the phrasing of the verse, "I am Kel Shaddai," implies that in this statement, the Ribono shel Olam revealed a new concept that did not exist in the world previously. This proves that the revelation of the name Shaddai that Avraham received was higher than that which "I said to My world, 'Dai'" (as will be explained in section 3).

Another proof for this: the Gemara states that Avraham recognized his Creator at age three. The Rambam states he recognized his Creator at age forty, and another opinion (in the Midrash) says age forty-eight. It is explained elsewhere that this is not a factual dispute, as there are many levels of such recognition. Based on this, if we were to say that by the Ribono shel Olam saying "I am Kel Shaddai" to Avraham, only the Elokus within creation was revealed (that "I said to My world, 'Dai'"), we would have to say that the Ribono shel Olam saying this to Avraham (when he was ninety-nine years old), despite his having recognized his Creator many years prior, means that previously he recognized his Creator through the necessity of his intellect, etc., which is not comparable to the knowledge and recognition that comes through a revelation from above. But the fact that the Ribono shel Olam said to him, "I am Kel Shaddai" (using language of novelty), implies that in this statement, the Ribono shel Olam revealed a new matter that he had not known before.

3) The concept is, as explained in the *drushim*, that there are two interpretations of the name Shaddai: "I said to My world, 'Dai'" and "I am sufficient (*dai*) in My Elokus for every creature (*kol beriah*)". The difference between these two interpretations (concepts) lies both in the meaning of the word "dai" and in the subject to which the word "dai" refers. According to the interpretation that the name Shaddai is "I said to My world, 'Dai'," the word "dai" refers to the world ("I said to My *world* 'Dai'"), and the meaning of "dai" is limitation—that the world should be limited and not continue to expand. According to the interpretation that the name Shaddai is "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature," the word "dai" refers to Elokus ("I am sufficient *in My Elokus* for every creature"), and the meaning of "dai" is a great abundance that is sufficient and enough for every creature. Similar to the expression, "sufficient for his need, which he lacks"—a great abundance that fills every lack, even "a horse to ride upon and a servant to run before him". [And although "sufficient for his need" is also within limitation—"sufficient for his need, and you are not commanded to make him rich"—the emphasis in "sufficient for his need" is not on the limitation, but on the abundance that fills every lack.] This is the novelty in "I appeared to the Avos as Kel Shaddai," beginning with Avraham, to whom it was said, "I am Kel Shaddai," in contrast to the name Shaddai that existed at the creation of the world: the name Shaddai that existed at the creation of the world is based on "I said to My world, 'Dai'," while the name Shaddai that was revealed to Avraham [and likewise to Yitzchak and Yaakov] is based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature."

4) The revelation of Kel Shaddai that was revealed to the Avos, based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature," is still lower than the revelation of the name Havayah, as it is written: "I appeared to the Avos as Kel Shaddai, but by My name Havayah I was not known to them." It is explained in the *drushim*<17> that the revelation of the name Shaddai based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" also includes the matter of miracles. Since the matter of "sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" is similar to "sufficient for his need," which means *everything* he lacks, it is understood that even when creatures ("every creature") need a miracle, the Elokus of the name Shaddai is sufficient to fill even this lack. However, the miracles that stem from the name Shaddai are miracles that are clothed in nature, whereas the miracles that transcend nature are drawn down from the name Havayah.

We must understand: seemingly, the fact that creatures need a miracle is (not only to save them in their time of distress, etc., but also) so that there will be a revelation of Elokus in the world. Since in miracles clothed in nature there is room to err and think they are nature, and the essence of the revelation of Elokus is in miracles that transcend nature, then "sufficient for his need" (seemingly) also includes miracles that transcend nature. Yet, the drawing down of miracles that transcend nature is specifically through the revelation of the name Havayah.

5) This can be understood by prefacing that the three matters mentioned above—the name Shaddai based on "I said to My world, 'Dai'," the name Shaddai based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature," and the name Havayah—correspond to the *keilim* (vessels), the *oar* (light) that relates to the *keilim*, and the *oar* that transcends relation to the *keilim*. The concept is that the fact that the formation of the world is with measure and limit ("I said to My world, 'Dai'") is because the formation is through the *keilim*. The formation itself (creation *yesh me'ayin*) is from the *oar* [and although the formation is by the power of the *Atzmus*, who alone has the power and ability to create *yesh me'ayin*, nevertheless, since the *oar* is like the *Ma'or* (the Luminary), the *oar* also possesses the power of formation], but the formation as it is from the side of the *oar* is in a state of *bli gevul* (boundlessness). For the formation to be with measure and limit, it occurs through the *oar* being clothed in the *keilim*. This is why the name Shaddai of "I said to My world, 'Dai'" is in the *keilim*, because the matter of limitation in the world is through the *keilim* that limit the (expansion of the) *oar*. And the matter of the name Shaddai based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" is in the *oar*, *that the expansion and revelation* of Elokus—the matter of the *oar*—is sufficient and enough for every creature.

It can be said that the intent of "every creature" is both for the most lofty creatures, whose lack is filled and satisfied by the expansion and revelation of Elokus, and for the lowest creatures, including those that have no merit and are merely "creatures" (*briyos*) in general, in whom the expansion and revelation is also drawn. It can be said that from the phrasing "sufficient in My Elokus for every *creature*," it is implied that the main emphasis in "sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" is that it is sufficient even for the lowest creatures who are merely "creatures." The explanation for this is that the fact that it is sufficient in His Elokus even for the most lofty creatures is not such a great novelty or elevation. Since even the most lofty creatures are limited, the revelation that is sufficient for them is also a limited revelation. The main novelty and elevation is that it is sufficient in His Elokus even for the lowest creatures; the fact that the *oar* (the expansion and revelation of Elokus) is drawn even into the lowest creatures, below whom there is no lower, indicates that it is *Ein Sof* and *bli gevul*, which is why it is drawn down lower and lower to infinity.

It can be said that this is what is stated in the maamar (cited above in section 2) that the revelation of the name Shaddai that Avraham received was a new revelation that did not exist previously, even in concealment, because the creation of the world with measure and limit is through the *oar* being clothed in *keilim* that *limit* the expansion of the *oar*, whereas "sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" (whose expansion is *bli gevul*) is through the revelation of the *oar* of *bli gevul* that transcends clothing in *keilim*.

6) Now, from the fact that both matters—"I said to My world, 'Dai'" and "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature"—are in the same name (Shaddai), it is understood that they relate to one another. For the fact that the matter of "sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" is through the revelation of the *oar* of *bli gevul* that transcends clothing in *keilim* is [not that the *oar* itself is revealed as it is in its place, above the *keilim*, for then the measurement and limitation of the world would be nullified, but rather], that even though the *oar* is clothed in *keilim*, which creates a limitation in its expansion regarding the formation ("I said to My world, 'Dai'"), nevertheless, regarding the revelation of Elokus, its expansion is *bli gevul* (as it is from its own side, above clothing in *keilim*): "sufficient in My Elokus for every creature."

**To add:** The fact that the revelation of the *oar* that transcends the *keilim* is in such a way that the revelation *of the oar that is clothed* is sufficient for every creature is because even the *oar* itself that transcends the *keilim* (of the name Shaddai) is an *oar* that relates to the worlds: "sufficient in My Elokus *for every creature*." As explained in the *drushim*, the name Shaddai based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" is at the source of the *hishtalshelus* (chain of descent); in the matter of the *Sefiros*, it is the *chitzoniyus* (externality) of *Keter*, which is the root of the *ne'atzalim* (emanated beings), and in the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*, it is the *oar* that measured within itself to shine with measure and limit. Therefore, after an *oar* was drawn from it that is the actual source for bringing the creatures into being (*oar* clothed in *keilim*), the revelation of this *oar* (which transcends *hishtalshelus* but relates to *hishtalshelus*) does not negate the illumination but, on the contrary, shines within it [and through it—within the creatures that were formed from it] and effects an elevation within it.

7) This is the difference between the name Shaddai and the name Havayah: the name Shaddai relates to the worlds, as mentioned, while the name Havayah transcends relation to the worlds. As our Sages said, "Before the world was created, He and His name were alone." The fact that His name [the name Havayah] existed before the world was created is because it transcends relation to the worlds. Therefore, even after the world was created, when the revelation of the name Havayah shines in the world, the manner of its revelation is (not according to the manner of the world, but) that the *oar* itself is revealed. Based on this, we can explain the reason why the miracles that stem from the name Shaddai are clothed in nature, while the miracles that stem from the name Havayah transcend nature (as mentioned in section 4): the *oar* that relates to the worlds has a manner of revelation where it shines and acts within the illumination that serves as the source for bringing the creatures into being; therefore, the miracles that stem from the name Shaddai based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature" are clothed in nature. The *oar* that transcends relation to the worlds (Havayah) has a manner of revelation where the *oar* itself is revealed. Therefore, the miracles that stem from the name Havayah transcend nature.

Based on this, we can explain the reason why the revelation of Elokus through miracles that transcend nature is above "sufficient for his need" (see section 4 above). In the *oar* that relates to the worlds, it is appropriate to say that when the *oar* does not shine in the world, the world is lacking, and when it shines in revelation, the lack is filled. But the fact that the *oar* that transcends relation to the worlds does not shine in the world is not a lack in the world, since this *oar* does not relate to it [and the drawing down of this *oar* is not the filling of a lack, but rather wealth]. Since the miracles that transcend nature are drawn from the *oar* that transcends relation to the worlds, the revelation of these miracles is not the filling of a lack, but wealth.

8) The maamar continues: based on the above, that the revelation of Kel Shaddai to the Avos is higher than the name Shaddai that existed at the creation of the world—for the name Shaddai at the creation of the world is based on "I said to My world, 'Dai'," and the name Shaddai revealed to the Avos is based on "I am sufficient in My Elokus for every creature," from which miracles that transcend nature are drawn (though they are clothed in nature)—we must understand even further the precision (at the beginning of the maamar) that specifically the name Havayah is the "true attribute." How can it be said that the *oar* that transcends worlds (and only relates to worlds) is not true, *chas v'shalom*?

The maamar explains this by prefacing that regarding the *hashra'as haShechinah* (indwelling of the Divine Presence) in the Beis HaMikdash, which was in a physical place, there are two opinions. The opinion of the Rambam [and this is also the opinion of the Rav Saadia Gaon, the Radak, and others, as brought in the maamar] is that since the Ribono shel Olam is not within the category of place, it is not appropriate to say that the *hashra'as haShechinah* was in the place of the Mikdash itself (in a manner of clothing), but rather that the place of the Mikdash was prepared to be a medium through which there would be a revelation of Elokus *for Yisrael* who came there. Similar to writing intellect through the fingers of the hand, where the hand is only a medium through which the intellect is drawn (in a way of "passing through"), even though the hand itself does not grasp the intellect. The opinion of the Ramban is that the *hashra'as haShechinah* in the Beis HaMikdash was in a manner of clothing, that the revelation was in the Mikdash itself. Similar to the dwelling of the intellect in the brain, where the intellect is *clothed* in the brain and the brain *grasps* the intellect.
