These two matters ("stood in its image before the Holy King" and that it was in ultimate *dveikus*) are two levels in the *bitul* of the *neshamah*. "Stood... before the Holy King" is like standing before a King. Although when one stands before a King he is in a state of ultimate *bitul*—for "he who points before the King, it becomes the opposite of his life"—nevertheless, from his own perspective, he is an existence, and his *bitul* is only because he is standing before the King. And the fact that the *neshamah* is in ultimate *dveikus* etc. is that it is not an existence for itself.

The *maamar* adds, after saying that the *neshamah* before its descent was in ultimate *dveikus* etc., that it was in a single will for Hashem alone, and there was no stranger with Him. It must be understood: after saying that it is in ultimate *dveikus*, what does he add and innovate by saying it has no "strange will"?

This can be explained according to what is in the Gemara: "Yaakov sought to reveal the end of days to his children, and the *Shechinah* withdrew from him. He said, 'Perhaps, G-d forbid, there is a blemish in my bed...' His children said to him... 'Just as there is only One in your heart, so too there is only One in our hearts.'" It must be understood: the *bitul* of the Tribes ("there is only One in our hearts") is at a lower level than the *bitul* of Yaakov [as is understood from the fact that Yaakov (the attribute of truth) was in doubt whether there was a blemish in his bed], and the language "just as... so too" indicates that both matters are equal. It must also be understood: what they said, "there is only One in your heart"—the *bitul* of the Patriarchs, they are the Chariot [and all the more so Yaakov, the chosen of the Patriarchs], was in all the limbs of the body. Why did they say "there is only One in your *heart*"? Furthermore, it is not understood, for the meaning of "*b'libcha*" (in your heart) is what is inside your heart [similar to the interpretation of "*b'kol*" (in a voice)], which implies that even in Yaakov's heart, the matter of "only One" is only in the interiority of the heart. The Mitteler Rebbe explains this in *Shaarei Teshuvah*, prefacing the difference between the *bitul* of speech to thought and the *bitul* of thought to the intellect. The *bitul* of speech to thought is not ultimate *bitul*. Although for speech to be ordered, it is because speech receives from thought—for in addition to the fact that before speech one must order in his thought how to speak [as is known regarding the matter of "He said four times to himself"], even at the time of speech itself, thought must illuminate the speech so that it will be according to the instruction of the thought—nevertheless, speech in its essence is separate from thought, and its *bitul* to thought is only regarding the fact that the order of the letters of speech should be according to the instruction of the thought. It is like the *bitul* of the Chariot to the Rider, where the Chariot is an existence for itself, and the *bitul* of the horses (Chariot) to the Rider is only regarding the fact that their movement should be according to the will of the Rider. This is in contrast to the *bitul* of the letters of thought to the intellect, which is (not only regarding the order of the letters but also) in their very existence, for the letters of thought exist specifically through the intellect (or the attributes) that is clothed in them [and the fact that thought wanders constantly is because the intellect and attributes are felt constantly in man]. Furthermore, the essence of the letters of thought is that they receive from the intellect or the attributes that are clothed in them. The matter is that the difference between thought and speech is also in the manner of their connection to the intellect and attributes that are clothed in them. Thought is the special garment, and speech is the separate garment. The matter of speech is that man clothes his intellect and attributes in an existence (letters) that is outside of him. Therefore, even though for speech to exist (from the perspective of the speaking man) it must receive from his intellect and attributes, nevertheless, once the speech exists, it is also heard by others and is separate from the speaking man. The essence of the letters of thought is that the intellect and attributes of the thinking man are revealed through them; therefore, even after the letters of thought exist, they do not go outside of him. Now, in the *bitul* of the letters of thought to the intellect, there are two levels in general. When one thinks the intellect in its letters [and all the more so when he thinks how to influence the intellect to others, and as mentioned above, that before speech one must order in his thought how to speak], in this thought, the letters are felt, but they are unified with the intellect, as mentioned above. And when one thinks the intellect itself ["thought within thought"], in this thought, the letters are not felt, because they are included and nullified in the intellect. This is what the Tribes said to Yaakov, that "there is only One in your heart" (*b'libcha* specifically), for the matter of "only One" is that there is no existence whatsoever besides Him, not even an existence that is nullified to Him. Therefore they said "there is only One in your heart" (even though the Patriarchs are the Chariot and the *bitul* of the Chariot is in all the limbs), because this matter of "only One" is specifically in his heart. And in his heart itself—in the interiority of the heart [as mentioned above, that the meaning of "*b'libcha*" is inside the heart], for at the level of the heart, even in the heart of the higher man, since there is the matter of the attributes there, even though "He and His life are one," this is not the true matter of "only One." Therefore they said "*b'libcha*," inside your heart, that in the point of the heart, the simple unity shines—"only One."

Accordingly, this can be explained: after the *maamar* says that the *neshamah* while it is above "stood in its image before the Holy King" and (furthermore) that it was in ultimate *dveikus* etc., it adds that it was in a single will for Hashem alone, and there was no stranger with Him. For the intent of "stranger" (here) is also an existence that is nullified in the ultimate sense (like the *bitul* of the letters of thought included in the intellect). And this is what it says, "and there was no stranger with Him"—that the *neshamah* while it was above, the revelation of the point of the heart shone within it, and from the perspective of this revelation, there is no stranger with Him; "there is only One in your heart."

**i)** According to the above, it must be understood what the Tribes said, "so too there is only One in our hearts." The Tribes are in *Biah* [as explained in many places the difference between the Patriarchs and the Tribes, that the Patriarchs are in *Atzilus* and the Tribes are in *Biah*], and the *bitul* in *Biah* is the *bitul* of *Yichuda Tataah* (lower even than the *bitul* of the Chariot of *Atzilus*). And nevertheless, they said "there is only One in our hearts," for the matter of "only One" is that there is no existence whatsoever besides Him (higher even than the Chariot of *Atzilus*). Furthermore, they said "*Just as* there is only One in your heart, *so too* there is only One in our hearts," that the matter of "only One" in them and the matter of "only One" in Yaakov are both equal. The Mitteler Rebbe explains in *Shaarei Teshuvah* there that the root of all the differences in the order of *hishtalshelus* [including the difference between *Yichuda Tataah* of *Biah* and *Yichuda Ilaah* of *Atzilus*, and higher still, the *bitul* of "only One"] is in the will regarding the worlds, that it arose in His simple will to emanate and create. And the root of the *neshamos* is in the *atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* itself, above the simple will ["With whom did He consult? With the *neshamos* of the *tzaddikim*"]. And since regarding the *atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* (the root of the *neshamos*) all matters are equal, therefore the matter of "only One" in Yaakov is also drawn down into the Tribes, the *neshamos* of *Biah*.

Accordingly, it can be said that the fact that the *maamar* elaborates on the greatness of the elevation of the *neshamah* while it is above, up to the elevation of "no stranger with Him" [even though the content of this chapter is that the essence of the *avodah* of the *neshamah* is specifically below], is (also) to hint at the power that the *neshamah* has in its descent below to overcome all obstacles and hindrances. The matter is that the fact that the *neshamah* was in a single will for Hashem alone and there was no stranger with Him is (primarily) from the perspective of its root in the *atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the will to emanate and create. [For in the will to emanate and create, since the will is that there should be worlds, there is a root and a place for a "strange will" (and not only for the level of the "strange will" mentioned above, an existence that is nullified in the ultimate sense, but also for a "strange will" in the literal sense, for the will that there should be worlds is that there should also be worlds of *Biah* down to the physical world). And the fact that the *neshamah* was in a single will for Hashem alone and there was no stranger with Him, it can be said the intent is that there is no place given even for a "strange will" (not even for an existence that is nullified in the ultimate sense), for this matter in the *neshamah* is from the fact that it is rooted in the *atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* that is above the will regarding the worlds, where there is no place given for any existence outside of Him.] And since from the perspective of its root in the *atzmus Ohr Ein Sof*, the matter of "only One" in Yaakov (the *neshamah* above) is drawn down in revelation also into the Tribes (*neshamos* below), therefore, even when it is in a body full of strange wills (strange in the literal sense) and physical and material desires that prevent the *neshamah* from Divine *avodah*, it has the power to overcome them.

**j)** After the *maamar* explains that the descent of the *neshamah* below is in order to clarify and refine the body and the natural soul and to illuminate its portion in the world, it adds that the essence of the *avodah* (of the *neshamah*) is specifically below. Since the body is full of strange wills and physical and material desires that prevent the *neshamah* from Divine *avodah*, therefore it needs *avodah* and tremendous exertion in a most difficult war. It can be said that the fact that he adds that the essence of the *avodah* is specifically below is to explain another matter that is achieved in the *neshamah* through its descent below—the quality of *iskafya*. For the fact that he says earlier that the descent of the *neshamah* below is in order to clarify and refine the body and natural soul etc. is that through its descent below it comes to the elevation of *ishapcha*, and the fact that below is the essence of the *avodah* (*avodah* and tremendous exertion in a most difficult war) is that through its descent below it comes to the elevation of *iskafya*. According to what was explained above (section z), that in the twentieth chapter he explains the internal point of all the matters in the *hemshech*, that the internal point of all matters in the world is these matters as they are in man, it should be added that in the previous chapters he explains (primarily) the matter of *iskafya* and *ishapcha* in the world that is achieved through the *avodah* of man, and in the twentieth chapter he explains the matter of *iskafya* and *ishapcha* in the *avodah* of man himself.

**k)** In the *avodah* of man there are three lines: Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*. It can be said that one of the reasons why the line of *avodah* specifically is called by the name *avodah* is because the empowerment for the matter of *avodah* in all three lines (*avodah* and tremendous exertion in a most difficult war) is through the line of *avodah*, the *avodah* of the sacrifices, and in the time of *galus*—the *avodah* of prayer. This will be understood according to what the Tzemach Tzedek explains in the *maamar* "And you shall serve Hashem your G-d," that this refers to the *avodah* of prayer. For the matter of prayer (prayer from the word *chibur*—connection) is to connect *Malchus*, the level of "filling all worlds," with the level of "surrounding all worlds." There are two matters in this: the prayer itself (the *avodah* of prayer of man) is to elevate *Malchus* to the surrounding light, "I will exalt You, my G-d, O King," the connection of "filling" and "surrounding" in a manner of from below to above; and the *hemshech* from above that is achieved through prayer is the connection of "surrounding" and "filling" in a manner of from above to below, that the surrounding light that is above *hishtalshelus* is drawn down in revelation into *Malchus*. It can be said that this is "And you shall serve Hashem your G-d"—for Hashem is "surrounding" and G-d is "filling," and the meaning of "And you shall serve Hashem your G-d" is that the matter of "and you shall serve" (the *avodah* of prayer) is that Hashem (surrounding) should be your G-d, your power and your life. And the fact that through prayer a unification of "surrounding" and "filling" is achieved, the Tzemach Tzedek explains there, for prayer is "a ladder set on the earth and its top reaches the heavens"—the connection of the *neshamah* in the body (earth) with the *neshamah* above (heavens). For the *neshamah* in the body is like "filling," and the *neshamah* above [which is the level of *makif* to the *neshamah* in the body] is like "surrounding," and through the unification of "filling" and "surrounding" that is in man, a unification of "filling" and "surrounding" is achieved. This can be connected to the matter of prayer in the simple sense, that man asks for his needs, for the fulfillment of the requests in prayer is through the unification of "surrounding" and "filling." For the fact that a *change* is made in the created beings below (that the sick person is healed etc.) is through the *hemshech* of the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above *hishtalshelus* (for from the perspective of *hishtalshelus*, it was decreed upon him that he should be sick), and the fact that through the *hemshech* of the light that is above *hishtalshelus*, specific influences are bestowed (healing of the sick, blessing of the years, and the like) is because the light that is above *hishtalshelus* is drawn down into *hishtalshelus*.

It is known that for the connection of "surrounding" and "filling" to exist, it is through the *hemshech* of the *atzmus Ohr Ein Sof* that is above "surrounding" and "filling." Similarly, this is the case regarding the *neshamah*, that the connection of the *neshamah* above and the *neshamah* in the body is through the *hemshech* of the essence of the *neshamah*, which in general is *yechidah*. This *hemshech* is through the *avodah* of "with all your might" (*b'chol me'odecha*)—"in every measure and measure that He measures for you, you should thank Him." For the fact that he thanks the Holy One, blessed be He, in every measure and measure equally is from the perspective of the revelation of *yechidah* [for the *neshamah* in the body is *nefesh-ruach-neshamah*, the *neshamah* above is *chayah*, and the essence of the *neshamah* is *yechidah*]. Accordingly, it will be understood that the *avodah* of prayer is the empowerment for the matter of *avodah* (*avodah* and tremendous exertion in a most difficult war) in all three lines, for the power to overcome all obstacles is from the perspective of the essence of the *neshamah* (as mentioned above, section t), and through prayer, the connection of the *neshamah* in the body (*nefesh-ruach-neshamah*) and the *neshamah* above (*chayah*), the revelation of *yechidah* is also drawn down.

**l)** It was explained above (end of section c) that the essence of the matter of *iskafya* (and *ishapcha*) and the *hemshech* achieved thereby is during the time of *galus*. It should be noted that this matter is also emphasized in prayer, as the Tzemach Tzedek explains in the aforementioned *maamar* that prayer in the time of *galus* is more sublime than prayer in the time of the *Beis HaMikdash*. As the Alter Rebbe writes that in the time of *galus*, *Malchus* itself is in the category of prayer. For in the time of the *Beis HaMikdash*, *Malchus* asked for its needs from *Ze'er Anpin*, its husband (meaning that the *hemshech* of the light that is above *hishtalshelus* into *Malchus* was through *Ze'er Anpin*), and in the

## B’s”D. Shabbos Kodesh and Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Beshalach, 11 Shevat, 5731

### B’s”D. Shabbos Kodesh and Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Beshalach, 11 Shevat, 5731

"I have come into My garden, My sister, My bride". In the maamar of the day of his histalkus and his hilula, the Rebbe, my father-in-law, the Baal HaHilula, cites the Midrash Rabbah (ad loc.) on the words "into My garden": "Into My *ginuni* (garden/chamber)—to the place where it was primarily at the beginning." The *ikar Shechinah* (the essence of the Divine Presence) was originally in the lower realms, but due to undesirable matters (beginning with the sin of the Tree of Knowledge), the Shechinah withdrew from below to above, up to the seventh heaven. Subsequently, the tzaddikim arose (beginning with Avraham Avinu) and brought the Shechinah down from above to below, until Moshe came, who is the seventh, and "all sevenths are beloved", and he brought it down below to the earth. This matter—the *hemshech* (drawing down) and descent of the Shechinah below to the earth—was accomplished primarily through the Mishkan that Moshe made, as it is written, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them." This is the meaning of "The tzaddikim shall inherit the earth and dwell forever upon it": the tzaddikim cause the Shechinah, who is called "Ad" (Forever/Witness)—"He who dwells forever (*shochen ad*), high and holy"—to dwell, meaning they draw her down so that she will be revealed below.

**It should be noted** that the Midrash there first explains the verse "The tzaddikim shall inherit the earth," and only afterwards states that the primary dwelling of the Shechinah on earth occurred on the day the Mishkan was erected. The maamar, however, *reverses* the order of these matters, citing the verse "The tzaddikim shall inherit the earth" only after stating that the primary *hemshech* was through the making of the Mishkan. It can be said that this hints that the fact that tzaddikim draw the Shechinah below ("and dwell forever upon it") refers not only to the *hemshech* accomplished by the seven tzaddikim from Avraham until Moshe (through the making of the Mishkan), but also to the *hemshech* that follows the *hemshech* of the Mishkan. Even after the *hemshech* of the Mishkan—which was an extremely lofty *hemshech*—the tzaddikim, for "Your people are all tzaddikim", draw down an even loftier *hemshech*. For after the *hemshech* of the seven tzaddikim, and especially through Moshe, by whom the Shechinah was drawn down to the earth, the power was granted to every single Jew to draw down even more.

It can be said that just as the *hemshech* of the Shechinah below through the making of the Mishkan was through all of Israel [as it is written, "Speak to the children of Israel... and they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them"—the command to make a sanctuary and draw the *ikar Shechinah* below is for all of Israel], and the statement in the Midrash that Moshe drew the Shechinah below is because the primary making of the Mishkan and the *hemshech* resulting from it were through Moshe, and through him the power was also drawn to Israel that through "And they shall make Me a sanctuary" there would be "and I will dwell among them"—so too is it regarding the *hemshech* of the Shechinah now. This is a loftier *hemshech* even than that of the making of the Mishkan, and the power within every Jew to draw down this *hemshech* is through the tzaddikim, the heads of the thousands of Israel in every generation, the "extension of Moshe in every generation", up to the Rebbe, my father-in-law, the Baal HaHilula, the Nasi of our generation.

2) **Now**, after the maamar explains (briefly) the Midrashic teaching in its entirety, it explains the details of the matter with greater depth and specificity. In the first chapter of the maamar (the chapter pertaining to this year), it explains the concept of the *ikar Shechinah* in the lower realms, and in the subsequent chapters, the *avodah* of "And they shall make Me a sanctuary" (and also the *avodah* within the Mishkan and Temple after its construction), through which "and I will dwell among them"—the drawing down of the *ikar Shechinah* into the lower realms—is accomplished. It explains in the first chapter that the reason why the *ikar Shechinah* was in the lower realms is because the ultimate intent in the creation and formation of the worlds is that the Holy One, blessed be He, desired to have a dwelling place in the lower realms. It explains that this desire of the Holy One, blessed be He, to have a dwelling place in the lower realms means that there should be a revelation of Elokus below (not through a *hemshech* from above, but) through the *avodah* of man, and that the *avodah* through which this revelation of Elokus in the world is accomplished is the *avodah* of *iskafya* and *is'hapcha*, as the saying goes: "When the *sitra achra* is subjugated (*iskafya*), the glory of the Holy One, blessed be He, is exalted in all the worlds." It explains that the meaning of "in all the worlds" is a light that is in all the worlds equally. This is the light that is above any relationship to the worlds. For in the light that is enclothed within the worlds, there is a distinction between the upper worlds and the lower worlds, as the Sages say: "He stretched out His right hand and created the heavens, He stretched out His left hand and created the earth"—the *hemshech* in the heavens (upper worlds) is from the aspect of the right, and the *hemshech* in the earth (lower worlds) is from the aspect of the left. But the aspect of "in all the worlds"—the light that is in all the worlds equally—is the light that is above relationship to the worlds. This is "When the *sitra achra* is subjugated, the glory of the Holy One, blessed be He, is exalted in all the worlds"—that through the *avodah* of *iskafya* and *is'hapcha*, the light that is in all the worlds equally is drawn down, a light that is above relationship to the worlds.

One must understand: on the face of it, the reason why the *ikar Shechinah* was in the lower realms is because the Holy One, blessed be He, desired to have a dwelling place in the lower realms. Why is it relevant here to explain that the making of the dwelling must be through the *avodah* of man, that the *avodah* through which the dwelling is made is the *avodah* of *iskafya* and *is'hapcha*, and that through this *avodah* the light that is above relationship to the worlds is drawn down?

The explanation for this is that the Midrashic teaching, "The *ikar Shechinah* was in the lower realms," contains two points: the superiority of the lower realms (relative to the upper ones), in which the *ikar Shechinah* specifically resided, and that the level of the Shechinah that was in the lower realms was (not just any Shechinah, but) the *ikar* (essence) of the Shechinah. The matters in the maamar (in the first chapter) are an explanation of these two points. The fact that He desired that the revelation of Elokus below (a dwelling in the lower realms) should be through the *avodah* of man emphasizes the superiority of the lower realms. For in addition to the fact that He desired that the "dwelling" (revelation of Elokus) should be in the lower realms, He also desired that the *making* of the dwelling should be through the *avodah* of the lower beings. Because the revelation of Elokus in the lower realms is drawn down through their *avodah*, they become vessels for the revelation, and furthermore, the revelation drawn into them is from the perspective of the lower beings themselves. It adds that the *avodah* through which the dwelling is made is the *avodah* of *iskafya* and *is'hapcha*, because through this, it is emphasized even more that the revelation drawn into the lower realms is from the perspective of their own nature. For in addition to the fact that the revelation drawn into them is through their *avodah*, the *avodah* (through which the revelation is drawn) is also an *avodah* that stems from the nature of the lower beings. And the fact that the *hemshech* through the *avodah* of *iskafya* and *is'hapcha* is the *hemshech* of the light that is in all the worlds equally is an explanation of the term *ikar Shechinah* in the lower realms, as explained elsewhere that the *ikar Shechinah* is the light that is above the worlds.

3) **To add**, these two aforementioned points are also understood from the language of the Sages "He desired... a dwelling place in the lower realms"—He *desired* specifically. The matter is that in the Zohar and Etz Chaim it is stated that there are reasons for the creation of the worlds [in the Zohar it is stated that the reason is "so that they may know Him," and in Etz Chaim it is stated that the reason is so that the perfection of His powers and actions would be revealed], whereas in the aforementioned teaching of the Sages it says that the creation of the worlds was because the Holy One, blessed be He, *desired* to have a dwelling place in the lower realms. The concept of "desired" is above reason; in the words of the Alter Rebbe, "To a desire, there is no question"—meaning that the concept of desire is in a place (level) where a question does not apply, because it is above intellect. The explanation for this is well-known: the Zohar and Etz Chaim speak of the creation of the worlds as it is from the perspective of the *giluyim* (revelations), and since even the most sublime revelations are defined by some definition, the desire to create something is because that thing corresponds to their definition (a reason). The aforementioned teaching of the Sages, however, speaks of the creation as it is from the perspective of the *Atzmut* (the Essence), and since He is not defined by any definition, the desire for the creation of the worlds in the *Atzmut* is not because there is some superiority in the creation, but because He so *desired*, above reason. And just as the concept of "desired" is in the *Atzmut*, the *thing* to which He desires is that He should have a *dwelling* in the lower realms, for the concept of a dwelling is akin to a person's dwelling, where the essence of the person dwells in the dwelling—meaning that in the lower realms there should be a *hemshech* and revelation of the *Atzmut*.

Now, it is known that the proof that the reason for the creation of the worlds is because He *desired* (above reason) is that in order for the matter of "so that they may know Him" (knowledge of Elokus) to exist, the creation of the upper worlds (the world of Atzilus and the worlds of the Ohr Ein Sof above Atzilus) would have sufficed, and the creation of the lower worlds would not have been necessary. On the contrary, the essence of "so that they may know Him" is specifically in the upper worlds. For the knowledge and comprehension of Elokus in the lower worlds (the spiritual worlds of Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah) is only of the *existence* of Elokus, not of His essence. And certainly this is true in the physical world of Asiyah, where even the existence of Elokus is not felt. The same applies to the reason "so that the perfection of His powers and actions would be revealed": in order for the perfection of His powers to be revealed, the creation of the upper worlds would have sufficed, for since the upper worlds are also an existence and their formation is by way of renewal (*yesh me'ayin*), therefore, by the Ohr Ein Sof bringing them into being, the power within Him is revealed (that it is in His power to bring a new thing into being). Furthermore, it can be said that the primary revelation of the perfection of His powers is specifically in the creation of the upper worlds. For since the creation of the lower worlds (Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah) is through the fact that the Divine power that brings them into being is *hidden* from them, in this action of the power (the creation of Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah), the power is not revealed. And certainly it is not revealed in the creation of this lower world, where the concealment of the creative power is even greater. From the fact that the spiritual worlds of Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah were created (at all), and certainly from the creation of this physical world, which has (in and of itself) no superiority—in the words of the Alter Rebbe, "a lower realm than which there is no lower"—it is proven that the ultimate intent of creation is (not because of a reason, but) because He so desired that this lower world should be a dwelling for Him, for His Essence.

It can be said that the fact that there is a logical proof for the concept of "desired" (above reason) and that the proof is from the creation of this lower world than which there is no lower, is because the creation of this lower world from the fact that He desired was [not in such a way that the concept of "desired" remained in His Essence and the fact that this lower world was created from the concept of "desired" is by way of separation, but] that the concept of "desired" is drawn into the creation of this lower world. [Similar to the pleasure in a person (for "desired" pertains to pleasure), for although pleasure is above all the powers, above even will, nevertheless (and precisely because of this), it is drawn into all the powers of the soul and even into the limbs of the body, to the extent that the expansion of the pleasure is drawn and spreads even into the essence of the foot: "A good report makes the bones fat"]. Therefore, the creation of this lower world itself (the fact that a lower realm was created than which there is no lower) proves the concept of "desired." To add: the very fact that the creation of this lower world was in such a way that the concept of "desired" was drawn into the creation is because in order for the intent of a "dwelling in the lower realms" to be completed—that the lower realms should be a dwelling for His Essence from the perspective of the nature of the lower realms themselves—they must have some connection to the *Atzmut* (even before they become a dwelling). Therefore, the creation of this lower world from the fact that He desired was in such a way that in the world itself (in its creation) there would be the concept of "desired," the will of the *Atzmut*.

4) **Based on this**, one can explain what is stated in the maamar that the reason why the *ikar Shechinah* was in the lower realms is because the Holy One, blessed be He, desired to have a dwelling place in the lower realms. For on the face of it, according to the explanation (in the maamar) that the fact that He desired a dwelling in the lower realms means that the revelation of Elokus below (a dwelling in the lower realms) should be (not through a *hemshech* from above, but) through the *avodah* of man, how is the concept of "desired... a dwelling in the lower realms" a reason for the fact that at the beginning of creation (before there was the *avodah* of man) the *ikar Shechinah* was in the lower realms? The explanation for this is that the fact that the *ikar Shechinah* was in the lower realms (at the beginning of creation) is that the creation of this lower world was in such a way that the concept of "desired" was drawn into its creation (as above), through which the preparation was made so that through the *avodah* of man it would be a dwelling for the *Atzmut* also from the perspective of its own nature (the world's).

It can be said that this is why the *ikar Shechinah* withdrew due to sins, because since a sin is the opposite of the will and pleasure from above, therefore, through sins, the *ikar Shechinah* withdrew from the earth, as it no longer contained the concept of "desired." Based on this, in the *hemshech* below to the earth through the Mishkan that Moshe made, there are two matters: the *hemshech* of the *ikar Shechinah* below—"I have come into My garden," to the place where it was primarily at the beginning—through which the world was made prepared to be a dwelling for Him, just as it was at the beginning of creation. And in addition to this, through the *avodah* of "And they shall make Me a sanctuary," the world also became a "dwelling" for Him, in that a revelation of Elokus was drawn into it (into the Mishkan, and through it into the entire world).

5) **Now**, it is known that every descent is for the sake of an ascent. The descent of the soul below is so that through this the soul will ascend to a loftier level than it was before its descent below, and the same applies to the descent (and the *hishtalshelus*) of the worlds: the fact that they descended from level to level (from world to world) until this lower world was created from them is for the sake of the ascent that is accomplished through this [that this lower world specifically is a dwelling for Him, a dwelling for the *Atzmut*]. From this it is understood that the same applies regarding the descent in this world itself, that the descent that occurred in it through the sin of the Tree of Knowledge and the undesirable matters that followed is for the sake of an ascent, so that the *hemshech* of the *ikar Shechinah* below now (after it withdrew) will be loftier than the *hemshech* of the *ikar Shechinah* in the lower realms that was at the beginning of creation.

**On the face of it**, one might say that the principle that a descent is for the sake of an ascent applies when the descent is by His will [in which case one must say that the will in the descent is for the sake of the ascent that is accomplished through the descent]. This is not the case with a descent caused by sin, which is the *opposite* of the Divine will [for through the power of choice given to man, it is in his power to choose the *opposite* of the will and intent for which he was created]. There is, on the face of it, no necessity that this descent is also for the sake of an ascent. And although it is impossible that man's choice should prevent the intent of a "dwelling in the lower realms" from being completed, G-d forbid, and man's choice only determines the *speed* of the completion of the intent—from which it is proven that the withdrawal of the *ikar Shechinah* through the sin of the Tree of Knowledge and the sins that followed was (from the outset) only temporary—this is only proof that the fact that the *ikar Shechinah* withdrew through sins was (from the outset) only temporary, and it is inevitable that afterwards there would be a *hemshech* of the *ikar Shechinah* below. But what is the proof that the *hemshech* below after the withdrawal is loftier than the *hemshech* that was at the beginning of creation?

The matter is that it is stated in the Midrash on the verse "Terrible is the plot against the children of men": the "plot" is hung upon him (the first man), that he brought death into the world (for "in the day that you eat of it, you shall surely die"), for the Torah preceded the world by two thousand years, and it is written in it, "This is the Torah: when a man dies in a tent." The Mitteler Rebbe explains that the fact that the "plot" is hung upon him (the first man) is not only regarding the death that came through the sin of the Tree of Knowledge, but also regarding the sin itself. For the fact that sometimes the evil of the *yetzer hara* overcomes man is because from above they incited the *yetzer hara* against him to bring him to this sin. [However, the help from above for the evil in man to overcome the good in him, and likewise the help from above for the good in man to overcome the evil in him, is separate from man and is not felt by him; therefore, it does not compel man, and the fact that man chooses good or evil is *free* choice.] And since the descent of the world (the withdrawal of the *ikar Shechinah*) that came through the sin of the Tree of Knowledge (even though the sin is the opposite of the will) was with intent, it is necessary to say that the intent of the descent is for the sake of an ascent, so that the *hemshech* of the *ikar Shechinah* after it withdrew would be loftier than it was at the beginning of creation.

Based on the above (section 2) that in the *ikar Shechinah* in the lower realms two matters are emphasized—*ikar* Shechinah (which is above the worlds), and *in the lower realms* (that the fact that the essence is in the lower realms is (also) from the perspective of the nature of the lower realms themselves)—it can be said that the advantage in the *hemshech* of the *ikar Shechinah* in the lower realms after it withdrew, relative to how it was at the beginning of creation, is in both matters: that the revelation of Elokus drawn through the *avodah* of man now is a loftier revelation than the revelation drawn through the *avodah* of the first man before the sin, and that the *hemshech* of the revelation in the world (in the lower realms) now is more internal.

6) **This will be understood** by prefacing an explanation of what was mentioned above, that although a sin (the sin of the Tree of Knowledge, and likewise all sins) is the opposite of the Divine will, nevertheless, it is with intent. The matter is as the Baal HaHilula says in the maamarim of the Chag HaGeulah: just as every thing below has a root and source above, the same applies to sin and iniquity, that there is a cause and a place for it above. He brings proof of this from the first sin that was in the world, the sin of the Tree of Knowledge. For on the face of it, it is wondrous: the first man was in the ultimate state of elevation, both from the perspective of his soul, whose wisdom was greater than the wisdom of the separate intellects, and from the perspective of his body, for the body of the first man was the handiwork of the Holy One, blessed be He, and the heel of the first man dimmed the orb of the sun. How is it possible that he sinned and transgressed the command of Hashem? To add: according to the teaching of the Sages that the reason man was created alone is to teach us that every single Jew is a counterpart to the first man, it can be said that the wonder of how a sin is possible applies also to every single Jew. For since the second soul within him is "truly a part of Elokus from above", and also regarding the body of a Jew it is said, "And You have chosen us from every people and tongue," and it is stated in Tanya that this refers to the material body, which appears in its materiality like the bodies of the nations of the world, and according to the known principle that the true nature of choice is in the *Atzmut*, it is understood that in the body of a Jew there is an advantage even over the soul, for in it (the body) specifically is the choice of the *Atzmut*. How is it possible that there should be sin in a Jew? The explanation for this (in the aforementioned maamar of the Baal HaHilula) is that the cause and the place for sin (in its literal sense, below) in the matters above is the "diminishing of the moon". And higher above, the cause and place for sin is the "breaking of the vessels of Tohu." Meaning, the creation of the world of Tohu, a world that "did not endure" (which is why there was the breaking in it), is (like) the opposite of the will. Therefore, it is a cause and a place for sin. And higher still, the cause and place for sin is the "first Tzimtzum" that was by way of withdrawal. For the Divine will is that there should be a *revelation* of light, and the *withdrawal* of the light (the first Tzimtzum) is (like) the opposite of the will [for although the *intent* of the Tzimtzum is for the sake of the revelation, the Tzimtzum itself is the opposite of the will]. And the concept of the opposite of the will (so to speak) that is in the first Tzimtzum is a cause and a place (through the breaking of the vessels of Tohu and the diminishing of the moon) for sin, the opposite of the will in its literal sense.

**It should be noted** that the proof that even the matter of the opposite of the will is with intent is primarily from the first Tzimtzum. For the diminishing of the moon, although *the Holy One, blessed be He,* diminished the moon, the cause for this was the complaint of the moon. And also the breaking of the vessels of Tohu, although the creation of the world of Tohu from the outset was in the manner of "building in order to destroy", it can be said that through the fact that there was the Tzimtzum, which is the root of the formation of the worlds, it was determined that the formation of the world of Tikkun would be after the building and destruction of Tohu, "building in order to destroy and destroying in order to build". And the primary proof that even the matter of the opposite of the will is not by chance, G-d forbid, but with intent, is from the first Tzimtzum. For regarding the Tzimtzum, it is impossible to say that there was a necessity, so to speak, in the Tzimtzum in order for the formation of the worlds to be possible, for the entire concept of the formation of the worlds (the word *olam* comes from *elem*—concealment) is from the perspective of the Tzimtzum. For the will for worlds in the Ohr Ein Sof before the Tzimtzum is that there should be worlds in which there would be a revelation of light. For since the concept of light is revelation, even the will for worlds that was in the light was (not for the worlds themselves, but) for the revelation that would be in the worlds. From this it is understood that the intent in the Tzimtzum [*withdrawal* of the light, in order that worlds should be formed whose nature is *concealment*] is not only so that through this the will for worlds that was initially in the light before the Tzimtzum would be completed, but that there is another intent in this, which is the opposite of the will that was initially. That is, the intent of the Tzimtzum as it is in the light (*that the Ohr Ein Sof* withdrew Himself) is that there should be a revelation in the worlds that will be formed from the Tzimtzum, and the intent of the Tzimtzum as it is in the Tzimtzum itself is that in it the concept of concealment that is above revelation is revealed, which is that the *Atzmut* is not compelled, G-d forbid, by the matter of revelation, and it is in His power to be (also) in concealment. Rather, the *revelation* of the concept of concealment [that the concealment is because the *Atzmut* is not compelled, G-d forbid, by the matter of revelation] is through the revelation that is drawn after the Tzimtzum. For through the revelation that is drawn into the worlds that were formed from the Tzimtzum, they can feel (in addition to the feeling of revelation) also the root and cause of the concealment that is in them, which is from the perspective of the power of the *Atzmut*. [And one should connect this with the concept of a dwelling in the lower realms, that this lower world becomes a dwelling for the *Atzmut*, for in this world is the essence of concealment, and through the fact that it is revealed that the concealment in it is from the perspective of the power of the *Atzmut*, it becomes a dwelling for the *Atzmut*.]

7) **And the same** applies to the *avodah* of man, that the fact that man can do the opposite of the Divine will [which is accomplished through the Tzimtzum, as above] is because the *Atzmut* is not compelled by anything. However, in this matter [the power given to man to do the opposite of the will], the concept of the power of the *Atzmut* is in the ultimate state of concealment and hiddenness. And through the fact that man serves Hashem and the *avodah* is by his own power and choice [for although the Ohr Ein Sof is in every place and there is nothing outside of Him, nevertheless, since He, blessed be He, is all-powerful, He created man in such a way that he could serve by his own power], through this it is revealed that the power of choice of man (even that he can choose, G-d forbid, the opposite of the will) is from the perspective of the power of the *Atzmut*.

One should connect this with the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov on the verse "And there was evening and there was morning, etc.," for it is stated in the Midrash: "'And there was evening'—these are the deeds of the wicked; 'and there was morning'—these are the deeds of the tzaddikim. And I do not know in which of them He delights, but since it is written, 'And G-d saw the light that it was good,' it is in the deeds of the tzaddikim that He delights." It is puzzling: how could it enter one's mind that He delights in the deeds of the wicked? The Baal Shem Tov explains that there is a benefit and advantage to light from darkness, meaning that the advantage of the superiority of light is recognized from the perspective of darkness, and likewise the superiority of the wise man is recognized from the perspective of the fool, the superiority of the tzaddik from the perspective of the wicked, etc. This is "And I do not know in which of them He delights," for since even the deeds of the wicked are a throne for the deeds of the tzaddikim, there is room to say that everything enters into one unity, G-d forbid; therefore it is said, "And G-d saw the light that it was good"—these are the deeds of the tzaddikim, for regarding the deeds of the wicked, separation is necessary [and based on this, he explains there the verse, "I thought of my ways and turned my feet to Your testimonies," as below in section 13].

One must understand: the fact that the superiority of light is *recognized* from darkness means (on the face of it) that through the *inferiority* of darkness, the superiority of light is recognized. How is it possible to say that because of this, even the deeds of the wicked (darkness) enter into one unity? It can be said that the intent in this, that the superiority of light is recognized through darkness, is that through the possibility of choosing the deeds of the wicked, it is recognized that the deeds of the tzaddikim are by their own free choice, *avodah* by their own power. And since through *avodah* by one's own power it is revealed that even the possibility of choosing the deeds of the wicked is from the perspective of the power of the *Atzmut*, therefore there is room to say that He delights in the deeds of the wicked, meaning that the pleasure from above [and likewise the revelation of His unity, blessed be He—enters into one unity] is also *in the possibility* of choosing the deeds of the wicked. And therefore it is said, "And G-d saw the light

## 10 Shevat, 5732

"I have come into my garden, my sister, my bride." In the maamar of the day of his passing and his hillula, the Rebbe, the Baal HaHillula, cites the Midrash Rabbah (ad loc.): "Into my garden" — into my *ginuni*, the place where it was originally primary. The *ikar Shechinah* (the essence of the Divine Presence) was originally in the lower realms. However, due to undesirable matters (beginning with the sin of the Tree of Knowledge), the Shechinah withdrew from below to above, up to the seventh heaven. Subsequently, the tzaddikim arose (beginning with Avraham Avinu, "Avraham was one") and brought the Shechinah down from above to below, until Moshe came, who is the seventh, and "all the sevenths are beloved," and he brought it down to the earth. This matter—the drawing down and descent of the Shechinah to the earth, which was accomplished by Moshe—was primarily in the Beis HaMikdash, as it is written, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them." It should be noted that the Midrash there speaks regarding the time when the drawing down of the Shechinah to below occurred—the primary resting of the Shechinah on earth was on the day the Mishkan was erected—whereas the maamar speaks regarding the place where the Shechinah was drawn down: the primary drawing down is in the Mishkan and Mikdash, and through this, a revelation of Elokus is drawn into the world at large as well.

2) The maamar continues by noting the precision of the verse's wording, "And I will dwell *among them* (b’socham)"—it does not say "among it" (b’socho), but "among them," within each and every one of Yisrael. This is the meaning of "The tzaddikim shall inherit the land and dwell forever upon it," for the tzaddikim are those who cause the Shechinah—which is called "Ad" (Eternal), as in "He who dwells eternally (shochen ad) on high and holy" —to dwell (drawing it down) so that it will be in a state of revelation below. It can be said that the reason the maamar brings the interpretation of the verse "The tzaddikim shall inherit the land..." after explaining that the primary drawing down was in the Mishkan and after explaining that "among them" is in the plural, meaning within each and every one of Yisrael, is to hint that when it is said that tzaddikim draw the Shechinah down (and "dwell forever upon it"), the intention is not only the seven tzaddikim from Avraham to Moshe, but every single one of Yisrael, for "Your people are all tzaddikim." That is to say, in the phrase "And I will dwell among them" (plural), which refers (also) to each and every one of Yisrael, there are two matters: That it is within the power of each and every one of Yisrael to be a dwelling place and sanctuary for Him, may He be blessed, so that the *ikar Shechinah* will dwell within him [in the exact same manner as it was in the Beis HaMikdash]—"And I will dwell *among them*." And also, that through this, a revelation of Elokus will be drawn into the world—"and dwell forever *upon it* (the earth)."

**To add:** The fact that the interpretation of "And I will dwell among them" (plural) as being within each and every one of Yisrael comes (in the maamar) as a continuation of the fact that the lowering and drawing down of the Shechinah to below was through Moshe, is because the power within each and every one of Yisrael to cause the *ikar Shechinah* to dwell within him is through the drawing down of the *ikar Shechinah* to below that was accomplished by Moshe. Just as the descent and drawing down to below that occurred at Matan Torah—"And Havayah descended upon Mount Sinai" —was in such a way that it granted the power that even after Matan Torah, they could draw down a revelation of Elokus to below [this is what our Sages meant when they said that at Matan Torah, the decree that "the supernal beings shall not descend below" was nullified; the *nullification* of the decree means that from then on, a descent of supernal beings below can occur]—so too is it regarding the descent and drawing down to below that occurred through the Mishkan made by Moshe [for one of the superiorities of the drawing down in the Mishkan over the drawing down at Matan Torah is that the drawing down to below at Matan Torah was from the side of the Above (and therefore the revelation was only temporary), whereas the drawing down in the Mishkan was in such a way that the Below (the physical materials from which the Mishkan was made) became a vessel for the revelation]. Because Moshe lowered and drew down the Shechinah into the Mishkan in a way that the Below became a vessel for the revelation, through this, the power was granted to draw down Elokus in this manner in the Beis HaMikdash, and also in the spiritual Mikdash within each and every one of Yisrael. And because the drawing down in the Mishkan was in such a way that through it a revelation of Elokus was drawn into the world at large, it is accomplished similarly in the spiritual Mikdash within each and every one of Yisrael: the resting of the Shechinah within the person ("And I will dwell among them") will be in such a way that through it, a revelation of Elokus will be drawn into the world as well ("and dwell forever *upon it*").

3) The maamar continues, stating that the reason the *ikar Shechinah* was originally in the lower realms is because the ultimate intent in the creation and formation of the worlds is that the Holy One, blessed be He, desired to have a dwelling place in the lower realms. It explains that this desire of the Holy One, blessed be He, to have a dwelling place in the lower realms means that there should be a revelation of Elokus below through the avodah of man, through *iskafya* and *isahpcha*. [It can be said that what is stated in the maamar—that the revelation of Elokus below is through the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*—refers (also) to the "desire... for a dwelling place in the lower realms." That is, in His desire that there be a dwelling place in the lower realms, there are several matters: that there be a revelation of Elokus below—in the lower realms; that the revelation of Elokus below should not be through a drawing down from above, but through the avodah of man—the avodah of the lower realms; and that the avodah through which the revelation is drawn below should be the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*—an avodah that stems from the nature of the lower realms.] The maamar continues, stating that the revelation drawn below through the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha* is a revelation that is above any connection to the worlds. [This is why through "And they shall make Me a sanctuary," there was a drawing down of the *ikar Shechinah* to below, for the *ikar Shechinah* is the light that is above connection to the worlds, because the avodah of making the Mishkan is the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*, and through this avodah, the light that is above the worlds—the *ikar Shechinah*—is drawn down.] As the saying goes, "When the *sitra achra* is subdued (*iskafya*), the glory of the Holy One, blessed be He, is exalted in all the worlds." The meaning of "in all the worlds" is a light that is in all the worlds equally, which is the light that is above connection to the worlds, and through the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*, this light is drawn down. 

4) The maamar continues in the second chapter (the chapter pertaining to this year) that the avodah that took place in the Mishkan and Mikdash is the avodah of *iskafya* that leads to *isahpcha*. Therefore, one of the avodos that took place in the Mikdash is the avodah of the korbanos, for the essence of the avodah of korbanos is *iskafya* and *isahpcha* (as will be explained). Now, the connection of this matter [that the avodah in the Mishkan and Mikdash is the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*] with what was explained at the beginning of the continuation (in simple terms) is that since the drawing down of the *ikar Shechinah* into the lower realms was primarily in the Mishkan and Mikdash, and the drawing down of the *ikar Shechinah* to below is through the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*, therefore, the primary avodah that took place in the Mishkan and Mikdash is the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*. However, at the beginning of the maamar, it speaks of the matter of drawing down the *ikar Shechinah* to below ("And I will dwell among them") through the making of the Mishkan ("And they shall make Me a sanctuary"), and in this chapter, it adds that even the drawing down in the Mishkan and Mikdash [after its construction, for this drawing down is more elevated than the drawing down that occurred through the construction of the Mishkan] is through the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha*. Therefore, the primary avodah that took place in the Mishkan and Mikdash is the avodah of korbanos—*iskafya* and *isahpcha*.

5) The maamar continues, stating that the avodah of korbanos is a spiritual avodah within the soul. It brings proof for this from the participation of the Kohanim and Leviim in their avodah, and their song and music at the time of the bringing of the korban, which are spiritual matters. [As is explained in many places, the drawing down in the korbanos was through the avodah of the Kohanim, and the elevation of the korbanos was through the song and music of the Leviim.] This is the meaning of "A person who brings a korban from you...," even though, seemingly, it should have said "A person from you who brings a korban," for this hints that the korban must be not only from the animal in its literal sense, but also "from you" (*fun ayich alein*), from the animal that is in the heart of man. It can be said that what is stated in the maamar—that the avodah of korbanos is (also) a spiritual avodah in the soul of man [even though the fact that the avodah of korbanos is the avodah of *isahpcha* is also true of korbanos in their literal sense, for when one takes an animal whose life-force is from *kelipat nogah* and makes it a korban to Havayah, this is the matter of *isahpcha*—turning darkness into light]—is related to what was said at the beginning of the maamar, that the interpretation of "And I will dwell *among them*" (plural) is within each and every one of Yisrael. From this, it is understood that just as in the Mishkan and Mikdash, the primary drawing down was through the avodah of korbanos [for even though the drawing down that was drawn into it through the construction of the Mikdash is a most elevated drawing down (for through "And they shall make Me a sanctuary," it becomes "And I will dwell among them"—I Myself will dwell), nevertheless, the drawing down that was drawn into it through the avodah of korbanos is even more elevated], so too is it regarding the Mikdash within each and every one of Yisrael: that even after the avodah of "And they shall make Me a sanctuary" (that a person makes himself a sanctuary for Him, may He be blessed), through which it becomes "And I will dwell among them," there must be the avodah of "A person who brings a korban *from you*," for through this, an even more elevated drawing down will be drawn into him—the *ikar* of the matter of "And I will dwell among them." 

6) This will be understood by prefacing what is stated in the maamar (at the beginning of the chapter), that the avodah that took place in the Mishkan and Mikdash is the avodah of *iskafya* that leads to *isahpcha*. For at the beginning of the maamar, it says that the avodah through which the *ikar Shechinah* is drawn down to below is the avodah of *iskafya* and *isahpcha* [for even though it brings there the saying "When the *sitra achra* is subdued (*iskafya*), the glory of the Holy One, blessed be He, is exalted in all the worlds," nevertheless, it speaks of both matters, *iskafya* and *isahpcha*. And on the contrary, the primary explanation (at the beginning of the maamar) is regarding the superiority of *isahpcha*, for through the fact that the darkness is *turned* into light, the advantage of light is created], and in this chapter (the second chapter), when it speaks of the avodah that took place in the Mishkan and Mikdash (after their construction), it says that the avodah in the Mishkan and Mikdash is the avodah of *iskafya* that leads to *isahpcha*. It can be said that one of the explanations for why the drawing down through the avodah of korbanos is a more elevated drawing down than the drawing down through the construction of the Mikdash (as mentioned in section 5) is that the emphasis in the avodah of constructing the Mikdash is the matter of *isahpcha* [that the person and the world are turned into a sanctuary for Him, may He be blessed], and the innovation in the avodah of korbanos is that in it, the matter of *iskafya* is (also) emphasized (as will be explained), and the drawing down through the avodah of *iskafya* is more elevated than the drawing down through the avodah of *isahpcha*. Based on this, one can add an explanation to why it explains (at length) the matter of the avodah of korbanos as it is in the avodah of man, because in korbanos in their literal sense, only the matter of *isahpcha* is visibly apparent [that the animal whose life-force is from *kelipat nogah* was turned into a korban to Havayah], and the matter of *iskafya* in the avodah of korbanos is more apparent in the avodah of korbanos in the soul of man.

The explanation of the matter of *iskafya* in the avodah of korbanos will be understood by prefacing that the superiority of *iskafya* over *isahpcha* is that the matter of *isahpcha* is that the thing remains in its existence, only that its existence is an existence of holiness, whereas *iskafya* is the matter of *bitul*, a *departure* from its existence. This is the reason why the drawing down through the avodah of *iskafya* is more elevated than the drawing down through the avodah of *isahpcha*, because through the fact that a person departs from his existence and becomes a state of *ayin* (nothingness), he awakens and draws down the state of true *ayin* (which is above existence). Based on this, one can explain the connection of the avodah of korbanos (even of korbanos in their literal sense) to *iskafya*, because the matter of korbanos is an elevation from below to above (as will be explained in section 8 and onwards), for the matter of elevation from below to above is that the lower being is nullified from its existence and included in the higher being [and in simple terms, the animal was burned (nullified from its existence) and included in the fire from above], and this matter [that the *yesh* is nullified and becomes a state of *ayin*] is the content of the matter of *iskafya*.

7) It should be added that this matter [the superiority in the matter of elevation from below to above (the nullification of *yesh* to *ayin*) that is in the avodah of korbanos] is related to what is explained at the beginning of the continuation, that the ultimate intent of the creation and formation of all the worlds is that through them, the formation of this lower world would occur, and through this, the intent of a "dwelling place in the lower realms" would be completed. The matter is that one of the reasons why the formation of this lower world occurred in such a way that first the supernal worlds were formed and only through them did the formation of this lower world occur (even though, seemingly, it should have been the formation of this lower world from the outset) is because the matter of a "dwelling place in the lower realms" is that the lower realms (the created beings of this lower world) should be nullified to Elokus, and this is accomplished through the fact that the formation of the lower realms is from the supernal realms. For through the fact that they grasp that the life-force within them was first included in the supernal realms and afterwards descended below (and even to a descent that is incomparable), the matter of *ratzo* (running) to be included in their root is created within them, through which they are nullified from their existence. It can be said that this matter is also hinted at in the principle that "every descent is for the sake of an ascent." For in addition to the interpretation that the intent of the descent is so that through it there will be an ascent to a level more elevated than the level before the descent, one should add another interpretation: that the intent of the descent (and the *hishtalshelus*) of the worlds [that is, in the fact that the formation of this lower world occurred in such a way that first the supernal worlds were formed and they descended from level to level (from world to world) until this lower world was formed from them] is so that there will be the matter of ascent, *ratzo*, and elevation. Similarly, it is regarding the Divine light that forms the created beings; one of the reasons why at first the light was included in the Source of Light—for this light is above connection to the worlds [and as is known, in the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum* (for the light before the *Tzimtzum* is (generally) a light included in the Source of Light), there was no place given for worlds]—and through the *Tzimtzum* (the *Tzimtzum* of removal), the light of the *Kav* was drawn from it (from the *Ohr Ein Sof* before the *Tzimtzum*), which has a connection to the worlds, and afterwards there were many more *Tzimtzums* (descents) in the light until it became a source to form the created beings (even though, seemingly, it should have been the drawing down of this light from the outset), is so that within the created beings there will be the *ratzo* and ascent to be included in the light that is above connection to the worlds, and through this, they will be nullified to Elokus with absolute nullification, *bitul b’metzius*. [As is known, the nullification that comes from the revelation of the light that is according to the value of the worlds is only a nullification of the *yesh*, and the true matter of nullification—*bitul b’metzius*—is specifically from the light that is above connection to the worlds.]

8) The explanation of the matter in more detail will be understood by prefacing what is said regarding korbanos: "a sweet savor (*rei'ach nichoach*)." Our Sages explained "a satisfaction (*nachas ruach*) before Me, for I spoke and My will was done." One must understand: this "I spoke and My will was done" applies to all the mitzvos, and yet it is said specifically regarding korbanos. The Alter Rebbe explains by prefacing what our Sages said, "Before the world was created, He and His Name alone existed." His "Name" is the aspect of *Malchus*, which is the source of the influence and life-force of the created beings, and the "Name" that existed before the world was created is the aspect of *Malchus* as it was included in the *Ein Sof*, blessed be He. For while it is there, it is not in a state of existence, like a light included in the Source of Light, which is not in a state of existence. Afterwards, the expansion of the light and radiance occurred to give life to the created beings from *ayin* to *yesh*. He explains that the fact that the light descended from its level (as it was included in the *Ein Sof*) to give life to the created beings from *ayin* to *yesh* is for the sake of an ascent: so that there will be from *yesh* to *ayin*—that is, that the *yesh* will be nullified and included in the light of the *Ein Sof*, blessed be He, in truth. Based on this, he explains why it is said (specifically) regarding korbanos "a sweet savor" (*nichoach* from the word *nachas ruach*), for the primary *nachas ruach* and pleasure Above is from the fact that the *yesh* is nullified and becomes a state of *ayin*, and the primary nullification of *yesh* to *ayin* is in the act of the korbanos. For the matter of korbanos is an elevation from below to above, that the lower being departs and is nullified from its existence and ascends (is included) Above (as will be explained).

**To add:** In addition to the elevation of the korban itself—that the animal that they sacrificed on the altar was burned (nullified from its existence) and included in the fire from above—an elevation is also accomplished in the supernal angels. As it is written, "My korban, My bread for My fires," the "fires" are the *seraphim*, and the korban is their bread and food, which adds to them strength and life-force to be nullified and included in the *Ohr Ein Sof* and even to be "absorbed" (*le'ishtaba*) in the body of the King. This is the matter of the song that took place at the time of the offering of the korbanos, for every elevation is through song. As our Sages said, "All those who possess song go out in song and are drawn in song," and it is brought in *Likkutei Torah* in the name of the *Sefer Likkutei Amarim* of the Maggid, that "those who possess song" are the angels [and one can connect this with the interpretation (in simple terms) that "those who possess song" are the *chayos* (living beings) that give song in their throats, for the angels are also called by the name *chayos* and *behemos*], and the meaning of "go out in song" is that through the song that the angels say, they go out of their vessels to be included in the *Ohr Ein Sof*, blessed be He.

9) **To add:** The fact that korbanos are the matter of elevation is also understood from the fact that they are called by the name *korban*. [And specifically according to the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov (on the verse "Forever, Havayah, Your word stands in the heavens") that the name of every thing as it is called in the Holy Tongue indicates (not only its matter but also) the Divine life-force within it.] For *korban* is from the word *kiruv* (closeness), that it draws close to Elokus. This indicates the matter of elevation. For in the drawing down from above to below, its connection with Elokus is through the fact that Elokus is drawn (draws close) to it, and *korban* from the word *kiruv* (that it draws close to Elokus) is that it departs from its place (its existence) and is included in Elokus. One can connect this with what is written in *Likkutei Torah* that *lehakriv* (to bring a korban) is the same letters as *leharikiv* (to cause to rot), for rotting is nullification in the absolute sense. One can say the explanation for this is that the elevation of korbanos is to the *Ohr Ein Sof* that is above connection to the worlds [and more than this, that the elevation of korbanos is above without end, as is written in *Pri Etz Chaim*, for it is stated in the Zohar "How far does the desire of the korbanos ascend... higher and higher until the *Ein Sof*"], and therefore the nullification of korbanos is a nullification of rotting, nullification in the absolute sense.

Similarly, it is in the avodah of korbanos in the soul of man, "A person who brings a korban *from you*," for the descent of the soul below is for the sake of an ascent, that there should be the matter of elevation from below to above, to depart from its existence and be included in the *Ohr Ein Sof*. And its elevation is through *tefillah*, for "the *tefillos* were instituted corresponding to the korbanos." And according to the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov, the fact that a person lives after *tefillah* is not according to nature. For because of the great *kavanos* in *tefillah*, he should have experienced *kallos hanefesh* (the soul's expiration), and the fact that he remains alive after *tefillah* is not according to nature, but a great kindness from the Almighty. It can be said that the fact that because of the great *kavanos* in *tefillah* one reaches (according to nature) *kallos hanefesh* is because *tefillah* is the matter of elevation to depart from one's existence and be included in the *Ohr Ein Sof*, similar to the korban that was *burned* and included in the fire from above.

This is what our Sages said, "One should not stand to pray except with *koved rosh* (seriousness)," and Rashi explains "submission and humility," for since the matter of *tefillah* is that one *departs* from his existence and is included in the *Ohr Ein Sof*—*bitul*—therefore the preparation for *tefillah* is also submission and humility, *bitul*. However, the *bitul* of *tefillah* is from the side of the closeness to Elokus, in which the elevation and wonder of the *Ohr Ein Sof* is felt, for which reason he wants to be included in it, and the *bitul* before *tefillah* is that he is embittered by his *distance* from Elokus. Therefore, the *bitul* of *tefillah* is a *bitul* of *kallos hanefesh* in which he is included in Elokus, and the *bitul* before *tefillah* is submission and humility. The Mitteler Rebbe explains in the maamar *Acharei Havayah Elokeichem Teleichu* and the Tzemach Tzedek also explains in his maamar *Zeh* that the fact that the *bitul* of submission and humility is a preface to *tefillah* is as our Sages said, "Whoever humbles himself, the Holy One, blessed be He, raises him up." It can be said that what is written in *Likkutei Torah* that *lehakriv* is the same letters as *leharikiv* is along the lines of the *bitul* that is before *tefillah*, for the *bitul* of rotting is that its existence is *spoiled*. However, through the rotting, the growth is afterwards created—similar to the humility before *tefillah* (through which the Holy One, blessed be He, raises him up in *tefillah*).

10) Now, in the contemplation that leads to submission and humility, there are two modes. In the aforementioned discourse of the Mitteler Rebbe it is explained that in order to come to submission and humility, it is through contemplating the magnitude of his lowliness and the greatness of his distance, etc., and that the contemplation should be with a good and precise calculation, called *mari d'chushbana* (master of calculation). [It can be said that the fact that he says the contemplation must be with a good and precise calculation is along the lines of what he explains at length in *Shaar HaYichud*, that when the contemplation is in a general way, his grasp of the matter is only in the aspect of *makif* (encompassing), and he can also deceive himself. And in order for him to have a true and internal grasp, it is through contemplation in a detailed way. However, after he contemplates the details, he must examine and contemplate the whole. [He says there that this is what his father (the Alter Rebbe) received from the Maggid as a complete explanation.] It can be said that the fact that he says the contemplation must be with a good and precise calculation is that he should contemplate the details. For in addition to the fact that a calculation (in general) is a calculation of the details, a precise calculation is that he is precise in every detail.] And in the aforementioned discourse of the Tzemach Tzedek, he adds that the matter of *mari d'chushbana* is along the lines of what our Sages said on the verse "Come to Cheshbon (calculation)," "Come and let us calculate the calculation of the world." The calculation of the purpose of the descent of the creation of the world and the purpose of the descent of his soul into this world, that the descent is for the sake of an ascent. And when he contemplates that he is not completing the intent for which his soul descended below, he will come to humility.

The difference between the two matters is that according to the explanation that the humility (the *koved rosh* before *tefillah*) is through contemplation of the magnitude of his lowliness and the greatness of his distance, the fact that the humility (*koved rosh*) is a preparation for *tefillah* is because through *bitul*, one reaches the most elevated levels, for "Whoever humbles himself, the Holy One, blessed be He, raises him up." And according to the explanation that the humility is through contemplation that the intent of the descent is for the sake of an ascent, the fact that the humility is a preparation for *tefillah* is (not only from the side of the superiority of *bitul*, but also) that the matter of this humility itself is related to *tefillah*. For in order for the descent to be felt by him (and that the intent of the descent is for the sake of an ascent) is through the feeling of the elevation and wonder of Elokus. And the more he feels the elevation of Elokus, the more he will feel the magnitude of the descent. And since his humility is from the fact that he did not complete the intent of the descent, in his humility, the elevation of Elokus is felt somewhat. To note what the Alter Rebbe wrote in his *Shulchan Aruch*, that before *tefillah* one should think about the exaltation of the Almighty, may He be blessed, and the lowliness of man; it can be said that the thought of the lowliness of man is not a separate matter but a continuation and result of the thought of the exaltation of the Almighty. Based on this, the fact that the *koved rosh* is a preparation for *tefillah* is not only from the side of the superiority of *bitul* but also because in the *koved rosh*, the exaltation of the Almighty—the matter of *tefillah*—is felt. However, the contemplation of the exaltation of the Almighty is regarding the lowliness of man, and therefore, through this contemplation, he comes to submission and humility. And the contemplation in *tefillah* is of the exaltation of the Almighty Himself, and the *bitul* through this contemplation is (not submission and humility, but) that he is included in Elokus (as mentioned in section 9).

11) **To add:** The difference between the contemplation before *tefillah* and the contemplation in *tefillah* is also in the matter of the exaltation of the Almighty Himself. The fact that the *bitul* in *tefillah* is that he is completely nullified (similar to the korban that was *burned* in the fire from above), a *bitul* of

## Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Bo, 10 Shevat, 5737

### Motzaei Shabbos Parshas Bo, 10 Shevat, 5737 

"I have come to My garden, My sister, My bride". Regarding this, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, author of the *yahrzeit* and *hilula* [of this day], brings in his well-known *maamar* for 10 Shevat 5710, that which is stated in *Midrash Rabbah* <a>: It does not say "to the garden," but "to My garden," to *My ganuni*—to the place where His *ikrei* (meaning the essence of His dwelling) was initially. For [at the time of the creation of the world] the essence of the *Shechinah* was in the lower realms. Due to the undesirable matters that occurred afterward, the *Shechinah* withdrew from the earth to the heavens, until it withdrew to the seventh heaven. Afterward, seven *tzaddikim* arose and brought the *Shechinah* down, beginning with Avraham Avinu, who brought the *Shechinah* down from the seventh heaven to the sixth, until Moshe, who was the seventh—and all sevenths are beloved —who brought it down to the earth. This is the general concept of the *avodah* of the *tzaddikim*—"And your people are all *tzaddikim*" —to draw the *Shechinah* down, as it is written, "The *tzaddikim* shall inherit the earth and dwell forever upon it." For the *avodah* of the *tzaddikim* is *veyishkenu*—to cause to dwell (*lehashkin*); *la'ad*—the *Shechinah* (the aspect of "He who dwells forever, exalted and holy"); *aleiha*—down below on the earth. The drawing down of the *Shechinah* below, which occurred through Moshe, was in the making of the *Mishkan*<b>, the essence of which is what is written, "And they shall make Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell among them," through which the drawing down of the *Shechinah* took place<g>. The beginning of the *Mishkan* was with the boards, which they made from *shittim* wood, as it is written, "And you shall make the boards for the *Mishkan* of *shittim* wood, standing." The concept here is [as explained in the *maamar* there based on the *Gemara* and the *Midrash*] that *shittim* is from the word *shtus* (folly), which is the *shtus* of the *sitra achra*, as our Sages of blessed memory said, "A person does not commit a transgression unless a spirit of folly enters him." The *avodah* is to transform, through *iskafya* and *it'hapcha*, the *shtus* of the *sitra achra* into the *shtus* of *kedushah* [as the saying goes, "His folly helped the elder"]. This is why the boards were made of *shittim* wood—because through the *avodah* of man, one makes the *shtus* of the *sitra achra* (*shittim*) into boards for the *Mishkan*. This is also the meaning of the name *kerashim* (boards), for *keresh* is the same letters as *sheker* (falsehood), but in a different arrangement.
